Name: No national definition for essential services access
Topics: Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: No national definition or indicator set for access to 'essential services' like water, energy, transport and digital services.
Questions: How should the UK monitor affordability and access to water, energy, transport and digital services?
Impact: Lack of common indicators hampers benchmarking and cross?regional comparisons; risks overlooking affordability issues for low?income households.
Evidence: There is no national definition of essential services. Neither are there country level, regional or local definitions. There are a variety of indicators of low-income people used but these tend not to be used in relation to access to essential services. The definition of lack ofaccess to essential services due to low income, where it exists, varies with the service in question (p4)
Report: Access to essential services for low‑income people – United Kingdom
Author: European Social Policy Network
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Distributional Analysis); Geographic Scope (UK); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Utilities)

Name: LFS response collapse distorts employment rate
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Problems with Labour Force Survey, including declining response rates, potentially increased non-response bias, and a failure to capture recently arrived immigrant workers mean the true UK employment rate is unknown.
Questions: What is the UK’s actual employment rate and how accurate are LFS figures?
Impact: Inaccurate employment denominator distorts poverty and labour-market analysis
Evidence:
“…we currently do not know what the UK’s employment rate actually is, due to problems with the Labour Force Survey (LFS), and that this issue has been ongoing for some time.  As the Governor of the Bank of England recently noted, 'it is a substantial problem [...] when we don't know how many people are participating in the economy', with broad implications for UK economic policy making.'" (p3)

“Between 2019 and 2023, the overall response rate collapsed from 39 per cent to just 13 per cent… the declining response rate is likely… introducing new response biases that are difficult to correct for.” (p4)

"As well as the possibility of a change in general response bias against workers, an
additional possibility is that the LFS has struggled to adequately represent recent arrivals
to the UK since 2020, a period when immigration from beyond the EU has been especially
high." (p4)
Report: Get Britain’s Stats Working
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (UK); Population Groups (Migrants); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Benefit take-up rates lack official data
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of official, timely data forces researchers to rely on experimental methods for calculating benefit take-up rates.
Questions: How much unclaimed benefits support is there in the North East of England?
Impact: Forces reliance on experimental methodologies to estimate unclaimed benefits
Evidence: Calculating benefit take up rates is notoriously difficult. The lack of official and recent data means estimates tend to rely on experimental methodologies and figures that are sometimes years old. Existing estimates are commonly created using previously published figures which are adjusted based on inflation and any new information on take up. (p8)
Report:
Missing out: Over £1 billion of
support is unclaimed in North East
England each year
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Pupil premium lacks longitudinal eligibility tracking
Topics: Misc
Headline: Absence of longitudinal monitoring in pupil premium hides pupils’ poverty trajectories
Questions: How do patterns of pupil disadvantage evolve over time and how do they affect educational outcomes?
Impact: Current systems can't understand patterns in pupil need, identify emerging trends, or intelligently design policy
Evidence:
"Implement longitudinal monitoring of Pupil Premium eligibility.The current system does not adequately track how pupils move in and out of Pupil Premium
eligibility over time. The DfE should introduce a longitudinal monitoring system that captures
these transitions, providing a clearer picture of how disadvantage evolves across a child’s school
life. This data would help policymakers understand patterns in pupil need, identify emerging
trends, and design more responsive interventions that reflect the dynamic nature of deprivation." (p52)
Report: Unequal Returns: The case for reforming the Pupil Premium
Author: Centre for Social Justice

Name: Small Understanding Society samples limit ethnic subgroup analysis
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Small sample sizes in survey data limit ability to report on all ethnic sub-groups.
Questions: How do experiences of persistent very deep poverty differ across minority ethnic sub-groups?
Impact: Hinders ability to provide detailed statistics on smaller ethnic groups’ experiences of poverty.
Evidence:
"The majority of this analysis is descriptive analysis that compares the persistent very deep poverty rate between people in households headed by people with different ethnic backgrounds. Given the small sample sizes for some sub-groups in the survey, even when data from different waves are pooled, not all breakdowns can be reported.” (p.57)
So far, the large sample size available for analysis in each wave of Understanding Society has allowed us to estimate the overall proportion, and number, of people who moved into and out of very deep poverty and who lived in persistent very deep poverty in the latest four waves of data only. However, to gain more robust insights into the experiences of smaller groups of people across the population, data from the past 10 waves of Understanding Society has been pooled for the following analyses. (p8)
""We show that a mismatch between Assessment Periods and when people are paid can create income volatility. This is likely to impact about one in six households in work, but without HMRC data, we cannot conclusively say that all of the month on month income volatility is because of a mismatched AP. In addition, our analysis is based on a subset of Universal Credit recipients, people on Universal Credit and claiming council tax support. This covers 46% of all UC households on average in the participating seven councils (measured on Universal Credit recipients at April 2022).
Report: Bangladeshi, Black African and Pakistani households at higher risk of very deep, long-term poverty
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities)

Name: Data on poverty premiums by goods and areas are outdated
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Data used for analysis of geographical dimension of poverty premium are old and not updated in a timely manner
Questions: What is the poverty premium for different goods (components) and at different geographical scales?
Impact: Reduced confidence in findings given temporal lag and infrequency of Census data.
Evidence: The data used is now relatively old, so it is our hope that all of the data sources can be updated in the near future. The census data is over ten years old, meaning that areas which have changed significantly in recent years will be under- or over-estimated. Likewise, the proportion of households experiencing individual premiums and the costs that they endure are also likely to have changed over time – whether as a result of changes in Government policy or commercial practices, or more widespread societal changes; for example, those brought on by the coronavirus pandemic and/or by increasing (although still not universal)uptake and availability of internet access and smartphones. (p20)
Report: Mapping the poverty premium in Britain
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Annual HBAI snapshots miss poverty persistence over time
Topics: Poverty Characteristics; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Annual  HBAI data only provides snapshots of very deep poverty, not persistence or transitions over time.
Questions: Who remains trapped in very deep poverty long-term? How often do people move into and out of very deep poverty?
Impact: Limits ability to understand persistence of very deep poverty and mobility in/out of it
Evidence: However, these figures, derived from the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP’s) Households Below Average Income dataset, only take a snapshot of the levels of poverty and very deep poverty each year. They do not give insights into people’s ongoing experiences of very deep poverty, nor their chances of moving into and out of very deep poverty between years. This means they cannot offer insights into people’s enduring experiences of deep hardship and the consequences of this. Without a better understanding of who is more likely to live in very deep poverty for a long period, we are unable to fully understand the extent to which social, economic and structural factors trap many people from minority ethnic communities in poverty. (p8)
Report: Bangladeshi, Black African and Pakistani households at higher risk of very deep, long-term poverty
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Transitions & Drivers); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty)

Name: Insufficient data for preventative vs reactive spending
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Insufficient granular data limits robust, causal comparisons between preventative and reactive homelessness spending.
Questions: How cost-effective are preventative measures compared with reactive homelessness spending?
Impact: Makes it difficult for the Treasury and other decision-makers to justify or target investment in prevention.  Have to rely on international or small-scale research
Evidence: “While the long-term cost-effectiveness of preventative measures is intuitively compelling, robust, causal evidence directly comparing it to reactive spending remains limited. In the absence of sufficiently granular data, valuable insights can be drawn from international examples and smaller-scale research.” (p. 5)
Report: A smarter approach to homelessness
Author: Institute for Government & Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Prevention); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: Northern Ireland's health + social care statistics can't be compared with other nations
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope
Headline: Northern Irish health and social care statistics are not readily available in a comparable way to other UK nations, leading to exclusion from analysis
Questions: What are the rates of unclaimed Attendance Allowance across the UK nations?
Impact: NI excluded from analysis entirely
Evidence: Many of the statistics used to calculate current take up derive from Stat Xplore, which holds benefit data for England, Wales and Scotland. Comparable statistics are not readily available for Northern Ireland and so Northern Ireland has been excluded from this current analysis.  (p5)
Report: People of pension age may be missing out on £5.2bn
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Other); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Poverty data missing for specific protected groups
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Breakdowns of poverty rates for specific groups are hard to get, (specifically by sexuality, gender reassignment, religion, and pregnancy)
Questions: How common is poverty for specific groups?
Impact: Poverty among some groups will be invisible / less visible.
Evidence: Data on sexuality, gender reassignment, religion and pregnancy are rarely collected when measuring poverty, and it is consequently difficult to report on experience of poverty among these groups. (p18)
Report: The Inequality of Poverty
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Population Groups (Other)

Name: Survey limitations hamper deep child poverty analysis
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Difficulty in capturing low incomes via surveys hampers analysis of deep child poverty
Questions: How many children live in deep poverty?
Impact: It is hard to draw confident conclusions about children in households on the very lowest of incomes
Evidence: Figure 6.2 shows that the proportion of children living with income below 50% of the medianhas trended in a similar direction to the headline poverty rate, with sharper declines from 1997 to2010 and sharper rises since. The proportion with incomes below 40% of the median is just over10%, broadly the same level as in the late 1990s and early 2000s. Difficulties in measuring verylow incomes in survey data make it harder to analyse deep poverty, so we should take thisfinding with a pinch of salt. (p268)
Report: IFS Green Budget 2024 – Chapter 6 “Child poverty: trends and policy options”
Author: IFS
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Children & Families); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: FRS/admin data integration lacking, distorting poverty rates
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of integration of FRS with admin data leads to inaccurate estimates of poverty rates.
Questions: What is the level of poverty in the UK?
Impact: Baseline and projected child-poverty rates may be biased up or down, risking misguided policy design and evaluation.
Update: DWP have been working on using benefits administrative data to replace survey questions about benefits.  Updated datasets since 2018/19 were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence: “Official figures on relative poverty and material deprivation come from the DWP’s Households Below Average Income data, based on the Family Resources Survey (FRS)… However, there is work to be done to improve the accuracy of these statistics… the FRS as it stands is likely to under-state the impact of any future anti-poverty income policy changes.” (p 7)  The authors argue that this is due to (1) the fact that survey respondents' answers may not be entirely accurate given the structure and topic of the survey, and (2) FRS may be under-representing the number of benefit claimants. "As these two factors should go in opposite directions, it is not obvious what the net impact on child poverty levels or trends will be." (p9)
Report: Turning the tide: What it will take to reduce child poverty in the UK
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Welsh ethnicity analysis combines all non-white groups
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Ethnicity analysis combines all non?white groups because of small samples.
Questions: What are the poverty rates for different ethnic groups?
Impact: Masks differences between minority groups
Evidence: “All non?white ethnic groups were combined into a single category due to small sample sizes.” (p26)
Report: Poverty in Wales 2025
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Data for modelling effect of policies on poverty in Scotland limited by small sample sizes
Topics: Geographic Scope
Headline: Limitations of data available for Scotland makes it hard to robustly model the effects of various anti-poverty options, ie to account for changing incenvtives on labour market behaviour
Questions: What policies would allow the Scottish government to hit its poverty targets
Impact: Modelling is more limited than it should be if robust policy impact analysis is desired
Evidence:
"It is worth noting that modelling always has some level of variability. This is
even more so in Scotland, where the data is more limited than is available at
the UK level. Therefore, the values presented are indicative of how a policy may
impact families in Scotland based on what we know at the moment. For
example, they do not take into account how people’s behaviours may change in
response to a policy (such as their incentives to take on more hours of work) or
events such as those that contributed to the high levels of inflation seen in
2022\. " (p9)
Report: Meeting the Moment
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations)

Name: Welsh minimum wage model lacks hours-worked data
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Minimum?wage modelling constrained because hours?worked data are missing within data shared by examined councils
Questions: What is the effect of an increase in the minimum wage to £15 on poverty in Wales?
Impact: Requires proxy assumptions, which may bias estimates of wage policy impacts.
Evidence: “The dataset used for this analysis does not hold the number of hours worked for all affected households… Complete earnings data is only visible within the data from one of the participating councils.” (p36).
Report: An examination of the impact of policy interventions designed to reduce poverty in Wales
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Local-authority data systems are not integrated and do not support early warnings of homelessness risk
Topics: Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Type: Data sharing; Standardisation and Harmonisation; Data linkage
Headline: Borough-level homelessness data are fragmented and cannot be linked, limiting early-warning insights.
Questions: How can data held by London’s 33 boroughs and partner services be shared to spot households at risk sooner?
Impact: Preventative support is delayed or mis-targeted; public costs rise when crises escalate
Evidence:
"Personal data on households experiencing or at risk of homelessness is largely held by individual boroughs, making it harder to use data across service boundaries to identify homelessness risk and intervene more effectively. This is partly because many people move across borough boundaries, but also because data sharing agreements with other services like health and criminal justice, which are not borough based, are hard to broker with  different partners.” (p7)

"In fact, even within a single borough, the key data that could prevent homelessness and identify vulnerable households is spread across several depts/systems." (footnote, p7)
Report: Pan-Approaches to Tackling Homelessness in London and Other Megacities
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Prevention); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: HBAI under-captures Scottish Child Payment
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: HBAI suffers from significant under-reporting of Scottish Child Payment income, which makes a flagship government anti-poverty initiative hard to accurately assess.
Questions: How has the SCP affected child poverty rates in Scotland?
Impact: Evaluation of the SCP is made more challenging, and the authors had to construct models that rely on unverified assumptions.
Update: Since the publication of this report, DWP have updated their imputation methodology used for SCP: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence:
"As noted above, the key data used to calculate poverty rates in the UK has failed to adequately capture the introduction of the SCP. The DWP, which collects this data, is working to resolve this in future but it remains hard to interpret child poverty rates in Scotland, particularly if you want to measure progress towards child poverty targets. We think it should be a high priority for both the DWP and Scottish Government to resolve this as quickly as possible.

It should be of significant concern for both the Scottish Government and DWP that the Scottish Government could invest over £400m per year in a targeted payment and for it not to be captured in these figures" (p23)
Report: Poverty in Scotland 2024
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Housing Executive can't disaggregate TA costs/outcomes
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services
Headline: Housing Executive cannot disaggregate temporary-accommodation costs or outcomes by user group, accommodation type or geography.
Questions: How do the costs and outcomes of different temporary-accommodation models vary across locations and user groups?
Impact: Decision-makers cannot judge value-for-money or prioritise the most effective accommodation models.
Evidence:
"Currently, however, there are gaps in the Housing Executive’s ability to break down costs by bed, by type and by geographical location. The Housing Executive also lacks outcome measures for people experiencing homelessness placed into different types of accommodation. Notably, although long
term hostels can provide wraparound care, the Housing Executive does not collect
data on the outcomes of people who access this support. This data gap presents
challenges when assessing how different types of temporary accommodation are
providing value for money. Our workshop highlighted that data is not readily available
in key areas including: user group; type of accommodation; and length of stay by user
group. Additionally, the Housing Executive’s systems do not allow it to easily track
costs across different geographic areas." (p32)
Report: Value for Money in Homelessness: A spotlight on temporary accommodation in Northern Ireland
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Disability data lacks condition-specific detail
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Disability data lacks specificity about the issues people really have
Questions: What is the prevalence, depth and drivers of poverty for people living with MS?
Impact: Lack of granularity on disability limits detailed analysis and understanding of conditions
Evidence:
“No National Statistics have been published on the prevalence of MS in the UK population…” (p?3).

"Moreover, estimates of poverty and the extra costs disabled people face... can only be measured properly in national data if the nature of someone's disability is also captured well. The standardised (so-calledharmonised) definition of disability forUK surveys (such as is used in theFamily resources survey) asks about‘any physical or mental healthconditions or illnesses lasting orexpected to last for 12 months or more’and the impact of these on people’sability to carry out day-to-dayactivities. It also asks about types ofimpairment, also using a harmonisedapproach, but not specific conditions(such as diagnosed conditions)." -- the authors go on to note that the Social Metrics Commission's approach is meant in part to take account of some factors not accounted for in the FRS, like housing and other fixed costs (p33)

The conclusion highlights “a distinct lack of evidence about the experience of poverty among people affected by MS.” (p?39)
Report: Poverty and Multiple Sclerosis: A Rapid Evidence Assessment
Author: Social Research & Statistics
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Disability); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Data-sharing restrictions limit benefit receipt
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Restrictions on data sharing limit receipt of certain benefits
Questions: Do people perceive automatic entitlement to grants and preferential tariffs favourably when it benefits them?
Impact: People don't receive the benefits they are entitled to
Evidence:
"Among our focus group participants, the use of data to facilitate automatic entitlement to any grants or preferential tariffs was perceived to be of great benefit. In each group, only some of those entitled to the Warm Home Discount (a discount of £140 from their electricity bills for people who receive certain income-based benefits) had heard of it, let alone received it. There was a strong belief that people who were entitled to it should receive it automatically, as it already relies on data sharing.“Warm Homes Discount, it does state at the bottom you have to tick some boxes to say that you, you know, you're happy to give consent for them to access your data on the records,” (Working age group, London)Participants were generally supportive of the idea of this type of data sharing where it was to
their benefit, as long as they trusted the organisation that held the data." (p45)
Report: The Poverty Premium: A Customer Perspective
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Energy Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Constituency-level statistics require approximation methods
Topics: Geographic Scope
Headline: Lack of desired geographical breakdowns of various statistics makes it necessary to use approximation methods to get various economic measures at the desired geography.
Questions: Various relating to the economic circumstances of specific constituencies in the UK
Impact: Although the researchers say they were able to manually use ONS geoportal lookups to get reasonably accurate approximations for what they wanted, this approach would be inaccessible to others with less technical grounding.
Evidence: We have good-quality age data at both the constituency and local authority level, but for a few of the metrics assessed in this report we only have access to the latter. In these cases, we use a best-fit approximation based on ward-level geographies to map from local authorities to constituencies. Box 1 details this method, using the ‘dual-coded’ age data as a test case for its accuracy. (p18-19)
Report: Painting the towns blue
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Other)

Name: Local economic data lack needed granularity
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope
Headline: Local-level economic analysis is often not possible with official statistics due to a lack of granularity
Questions: Various questions about local-area employment and economic security
Impact: We can't get a localised picture of economic security
Evidence:
"Quality of local-level data – Many key datasets lack the granularity needed to
understand regional gender disparities in employment and economic security." (p3)
Report: Gender, data and the labour market:Why data collection is not gender neutral
Author: Women's Budget Group
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Population Groups (Women & Gender); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: LFS weaknesses prevent robust productivity metrics
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Weaknesses of Labour Force Survey mean the UK lacks robust productivity metrics, so these cannot be included into economic forecasting of income growth.
Questions: How will real incomes change over the next 5 years?
Impact: Macroeconomic forecasting is missing a robust measure of a key variable of interest, which reduces the reliability of the projections.
Evidence:
"There are clear fundamental reasons to expect pay growth to fall back, however. Absent a sustained pickup in productivity growth (although labour force data problems mean the
UK lacks any robust measures here), and with the jobs market weakening, it seems likely
that wage growth will fall back from where it is now" (p11)
Report: Living Standards Outlook 2025
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (UK); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: FRS lacks geographic detail for LHA analysis
Topics: Misc
Headline: Lack of geographic detail in the publicly available Family Resources Survey (FRS) limits analysis of Local Housing Allowance (LHA) impacts.
Questions: How do changes in Local Housing Allowance affect households in different Broad Rental Market Areas (BRMAs)?
Impact: Researchers cannot model the impact of LHA reforms accurately across local housing markets.
Evidence:
“However, for setting LHA rates, the UK is divided into 200 Broad Rental Market Areas (BRMAs). Separate LHA rates are set for accommodation of each size group within each BRMA.
Therefore, to be able to model the effects of changes to LHA policy using a tax-benefit model, one needs to be able to identify any shortfall between the rent each family actually pays and the LHA rate applicable to that family. For the latter, it is necessary to know the BRMA in which each sample family lives.  However, for confidentiality reasons, the version of the FRS that is routinely released to researchers outside government through the UKDS has geographical information on each family limited only to the region or country in which they live rather than more detailed information on the BRMA in which they live.

The consequence is that, with this data, it is not possible to provide an accurate simulation of the effect on each sample household of a change to LHA policy. The result is that changes to the LHA have been subject to significantly less scrutiny from outside government than other major reforms to the social security system.” (p30)
Report: Stop the freeze: permanently re-link housing benefits to private rents
Author: JRF

Name: Without linkage between HMRC and DWP data, it is hard to know the reasons behind volatility in UC payments
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Type: Data access; Data sharing; Data linkage
Headline: Lack of access to HMRC data obscures a robust understanding of how assessment periods for UC affect income volatility.
Questions: How much UC income volatility is directly caused by mismatches between assessment periods and when people are paid?
Impact: Can't confidently attribute observable income volatility to assessment periods for UC; can't get a national picture of income volatility without HMRC data.
Evidence: "We show that a mismatch between Assessment Periods and when people are paid can create income volatility. This is likely to impact about one in six households in work, but without HMRC data, we cannot conclusively say that all of the month on month income volatility is because of a mismatched AP. In addition, our analysis is based on a subset of Universal Credit recipients, people on Universal Credit and claiming council tax support. This covers 46% of all UC households on average in the participating seven councils (measured on Universal Credit recipients at April 2022).  Enabling the use of earnings data, available in the data but not permitted for the purpose of this analysis, and allowing greater sharing of data on more Universal Credit recipients would help better understand the impacts and interaction of payment dates, assessment periods and income volatility.More importantly, greater sharing and allowed uses of Universal Credit data would support a wide range of additional research questions and deliver operational benefits, including better targeting of discretionary support to those most at risk." (p29)
Report: Cheques and imbalances: How income varies throughout the year for Universal Credit households
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Transitions & Drivers); Poverty Characteristics (Fluctuating Earnings); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: FRS sample sizes concerningly low
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: FRS sample sizes are concerningly low
Questions: Various related to poverty
Impact: Limits confidence in analysis.  If the trend is even lower response rates, the survey may not ultimately be useful
Evidence:
"Our assessment is that the 2022/23 FRS data is of comparable quality
to the pre-pandemic surveys, although the response rate of 25% of
households sampled agreeing to take part is worryingly low, as is the
expected drop in sample size to 17,000 in the 2023/24 survey." (p162)
Report: UK Poverty 2025
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Household data masks women's intra-household poverty
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Household-level data masks women’s poverty due to lack of gender disaggregation.
Questions: What are the long-term impacts of early years childcare on women’s labour market participation and financial security? How does poverty affect women differently within households?
Impact: Intra-household gender dynamics are not visible
Evidence: “There are some aspects that are not captured within the analysis. For example, we have not looked at the longer-term impacts of early years childcare on women’s labour market participation or workforce issues about the childcare sector itself, which is over 90 % women. There is also a challenge in how we look at poverty and income at a household level rather than having gender-disaggregated data. It can overstate women’s financial security and hide poverty.” (p.7)
Report: Poverty proofing the future of early years childcare
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intra-Household Inequality); Population Groups (Women & Gender)

Name: Wealth and Assets Survey publication lags
Topics: Economic Resources
Headline: Publication lag in the Wealth and Assets Survey limits picture of pandemic-era wealth changes and frustrates timely analysis of changes in the wealth distribution.
Questions: How did the wealth distribution change during and after the Covid-19 pandemic from March 2020 onward?
Impact: Policymakers and researchers cannot assess changes in the wealth distribution in real time.
Evidence: “The Wealth and Assets survey is only available with a time lag and the current published data cover the period up until March 2020. This means these data do not tell us how the wealth distribution changed during the pandemic, a time of huge economic disruption which saw falling labour incomes, volatile asset prices, significant increases in government transfers to households and businesses, and rising aggregate savings levels.” (p15)
Report: Arrears fears: The distribution of UK household wealth and the impact on families
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Economic Resources & Living Standards (Wealth & Assets)

Name: Data on two-child limit and benefit cap by ethnicity are difficult to access
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Two-child limit and benefit cap data that contain ethnicity information have a lot of missing information and had to be accessed via FOI request.
Questions: Which ethnic groups are most affected by the two-child limit and benefit cap?
Impact: Lower confidence in results; harder to obtain data than it could be
Evidence: The two-child limit andbenefit cap data come from an FOI request to the DWP (FOI2025/32658)and are for April 2024. All these data are provided at family rather thanchild level.In the FOI data on the two-child limit and benefit cap, 28.9% of thoseaffected by the two-child limit and 32.8% of those affected by the benefitcap did not disclose their ethnicity. (p8)
Report: Baking inequality in from birth:New analysis of the way the two-childlimit and the benefit cap intersect withethnicity
Author: LSE CASE - Stewart et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Small-area benefit cap data suppressed in Stat-Xplore
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope
Headline: Suppressed benefit cap data for small geographies (accessed via Stat-Xplore) limits accuracy of estimates of how many families are affected by it
Questions: How many benefit-capped families exist at MSOA level? (the study was trying to understand effects of benefit caps on families' ability to afford housing)
Impact: Somewhat limits the ability to draw conclusions at a local level, although this appeared to be a relatively minor issue for this research.
Evidence: “Due to statistical disclosure rules some MSOA counts are suppressed which means that the total number of capped families in our analysis is lower than the published national totals (around 17 % lower); this likely makes our analysis conservative.” (p13)
Report:
Capped and trapped (in the UK's
housing market): how the benefit cap
makes it almost impossible to find
affordable housing
Author: LSE CASE – Fransham et al.
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Other); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Comparable primary health/social-care statistics aren't available for each UK nation
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope
Headline: Comparable primary health/social-care statistics are not available for each UK nation.
Questions: What are the rates of unclaimed Attendance Allowance across the UK nations?
Impact: Introduces uncertainty/possible bias in GB-wide estimates; makes accurate assessment and comparison of data across devolved administrations much harder
Evidence: All countries making up the UK collate country-specific health and social care data. This data is collected and aggregated separately across each of the devolved administrations. Comparable primary statistics are therefore not available for each country. In order to overcome this statistical barrier, the ratio of the pension age population potentially entitled to Attendance Allowance compared to the pension age population receiving disability benefits has been calculated for England and extrapolated to other countries. Although this methodology accounts for differences in health and disability across the nations, as the ratio is applied to the number of residents currently claiming Attendance Allowance in each country, it cannot account for possible differences in benefit take up between countries. (p5)
Report: People of pension age may be missing out on £5.2bn
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (UK); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Other); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Family structure data unpublished across departments
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of data on family structure limits researchers' ability to understand how family size and breakdown interacts with a variety of issues
Questions: To what extent does family structure influence poverty and other outcomes?
Impact: Without regular publication, policymakers cannot measure how family breakdown contributes to poverty or assess intervention impact.
Evidence: “Any government serious about reducing the benefits bill in the longer term must champion family resilience, starting with changing our regressive, individualised tax systems, and penalties within the welfare system. Every government department should publish family structure data against key metrics to ascertain the extent of the problem..” (p 11)
Report: How to Get Britain Working (and Pay the Bills)
Author: Centre for Social Justice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intra-Household Inequality); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Local Authorities lack access to full DWP datasets
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Local authorities do not generally have access to the full range of DWP data, which frustrates efforts to join-up poverty relief initiatives
Questions: NA
Impact: Services to fight poverty are negatively affected by overly risk-averse data sharing agreements.
Evidence:
"Currently, Local Authorities are only able to verify a family’s welfare status on a case-by-case basis.
They are not able to use data to identify a target cohort and allocate provision accordingly. While
progress was made during COVID-19, with Local Authorities granted access to batch data in order to
target funds awarded via the Household Support Grant, the utility of incredibly rich DWP data remains
compromised by excessively risk-averse data sharing agreements." (p52)
Report: A United Nation
Author: Centre for Social Justice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Local AHC income data unavailable
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Absence of local-level after-housing-cost (AHC) income data limits accurate assessment of poverty gaps between places at granular geographies
Questions: How do poverty gaps differ when measured before vs after housing costs between small geographies?
Impact: Lack of AHC income figures at local level hinders comparison and detailed / nuanced view of inequality across the country.
Evidence: “Note that these gaps [between local areas in terms of their incomes before and after housing costs] would be smaller if we used an after-housing-cost (AHC) income measure, which is a better measure of living standards. For example, local-area AHC poverty gaps are around a third smaller than BHC poverty gaps. Unfortunately, AHC income data isn’t readily available at a local level. At the regional level, we know that AHC income gaps are smaller than BHC income gaps.” (p 10)
Report: Uneven ground: Assessing the state of UK geographic economic inequality facing the new Government
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: FRS/HBAI under-report income extremes
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Headline: FRS / HBAI suffer from under-reporting at top and bottom of income distribution
Questions: What is the distribution of income in the UK?
Impact: Some aspects of the income distribution are not fully understood
Update: DWP have been working on using benefits administrative data to replace survey questions about benefits.  Updated datasets since 2018/19 were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence: “The FRS/HBAI makes an adjustment for the under-reporting of upper-end incomes but it is nevertheless recognised that the top end of the distribution is not fully captured and reflected in the data. In addition, the importance of under-reporting of benefit income at the lower end is highlighted in Corlett et al (2018). ” (p33-34)
Report: Going backwards? The slowdown, stalling and reversal of progress in reducing child poverty in Britain during the second decade of the 21st century
Author: LSE CASE – Vizard et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Distributional Analysis); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: SMC poverty measure impractical to apply sub-nationally
Topics: Misc
Headline: Social Metrics Commission (SMC) poverty measure is hard to apply at sub-national level due to a lack of data availability.
Questions: How can poverty rates be measured using more holistic approaches developed by the SMC?
Impact: Alternatives to (flawed) official statistics are limited in their use until data become available
Evidence: “Unfortunately, data availability means that at present it is not practical to apply this measure of poverty [the one developed by the Social Metrics Commission in 2018] at a sub-national level.” (p10)
Report: Child poverty and devolution in North East England
Author: IPPR

Name: Pricing data unavailable at multiple geographic scales
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Pricing data is not easily accessible for a variety of products / markets, and it is hard to get it at varying geographical scales.
Questions: What is the poverty premium for different goods (components) and at different geographical scales?
Impact: Lack of detailed data on poverty premium components made it necessary to use microsimulation techniques to get estimates for different geographies.
Evidence: “Our understanding… could be improved through collecting more detailed data on different components of the premium and to publish this data in aggregate form at various geographical scales. Data from government departments, local authorities and commercial organisations (for example, banking transaction data or insurance industry data) could all help to improve our understanding.” (p 14)
Report: Mapping the poverty premium in Britain
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Declining LFS sample risks youth inactivity bias
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Declining LFS sample size risks biasing estimates of youth economic inactivity
Questions: How many young people are economically inactive?
Impact: Potential mis-estimation of youth employment, unemployment and inactivity
Evidence: Much of the analysis in this report is based on the Labour Force Survey (LFS), a widely used government dataset for examining employment trends. The LFS sample size has declined substantially in recent years, prompting concerns that this may have introduced systemic bias, limiting the dataset's ability to produce accurate estimates of employment, unemployment and inactivity levels (Corlett, 2024). While the LFS remains one of the most comprehensive sources of labour market data, its findings should be interpreted with caution. In general, the trends highlighted in this report were observable before the decline in sample size and align with analysis based on alternative data sources and methods. (p5)
Report: Unlocking the potential of young people furthest from the labour market
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Other); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Employment data for homeless only in England
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Employment-status information on people owed homelessness duties is only published for England.
Questions: What is the employment status of people experiencing homelessness?
Impact: Policymakers in devolved nations cannot benchmark employment-related homelessness risks or design evidence-based labour-market support
Evidence: “England is the only UK jurisdiction which publishes statistical data on [employment status for those experiencing homelessness]" (p11)
Report: Employment and Homelessness in the Context of the New Economy following Covid-19
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: LHA surveys non-compulsory, may misrepresent costs
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Geographic Scope
Headline: Surveys used to establish Local Housing Allowance levels are non-compulsory and may not reflect actual costs in relevant areas.
Questions: Are families receiving adequate support through Local Housing Allowance?
Impact: Potential misestimation of local rental costs leads to ineffective setting of LHA levels
Evidence: “Landlords are not compelled to provide this data [about local area rental market prices]. This – alongside the fact that samples for some areas and property types are small, and that households already claiming universal credit are excluded – leads to a risk that LHAs are not always representative.” (p18-19)
Report: The homes that children deserve
Author: IPPR
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Price data inaccessible for poverty premium analysis
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of open access to price data limits measurement of poverty premium.
Questions: What is the poverty premium overall and in specific types of markets?
Impact: Without granular price data, the scale and drivers of poverty premiums remain uncertain, hindering policy and regulatory action
Evidence: The process of conducting the research also highlights a need for more openaccess to data on the prices that consumers are paying for services in a rangeof different markets. While desk research allows us to obtain quotes for asample of customers or areas, based on key assumptions, it would bebeneficial to have more detailed data about the structure of different marketsand the value and volume of different services sold to consumers in differentareas and with different characteristics. This is particularly true of higher-costcredit markets and the insurance market. Implementing the CMA’srecommendation for a national measure of the poverty premium would help inachieving this. (p61)
Report: The poverty premium in 2022
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (UK); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Welsh poverty data require multi-year averaging
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Welsh poverty statistics require multi?year averaging due to small sample sizes.  Additionally, HBAI data used for analysis comes with a multi-year time lag
Questions: Various about Welsh poverty
Impact: Data won't give a "live" picture of poverty
Evidence:
"This report uses rolling 3-year averages to compensate for low sample
sizes of Welsh data throughout, unless otherwise stated. Where 5 or 7-year
averages are used, this is noted explicitly in an endnote or source note.
Most of the official poverty data (from the Households Below Average
Income (HBAI) series) used in this report ends at 2022/23. There remains
a Covid-19-related effect on the data collection, but methods gradually
returned to their pre-pandemic modes (see Annex 2 of UK Poverty
for details). The latest HBAI data for 2023/24 was released near the
completion of this report and is used in the overall poverty section only.
Any revisions in earlier data are also not presented, as the full dataset has
not yet been released." (p9)
Report: Poverty in Wales 2025
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Regional ethnic child poverty data unpublished
Topics: Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Ethnicity-specific child-poverty figures are not published at regional level.
Questions: What is the scale and pattern of child poverty among different ethnic groups within the North East (and other regions)?
Impact: Obscures disparities facing minority-ethnic children
Evidence: “No regional breakdown of figures for child poverty by ethnic group is available.” (p21)
Report: Child poverty and devolution in North East England
Author: IPPR
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Geographic Scope (Other); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: DWP shares data for only 45% UC recipients
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: DWP data sharing to local authorities is limited, which makes more than half of UC recipients invisible to councils.
Questions: How many eligible UC households are missed because LAs don’t receive data for UC claimants not on Council Tax Support?
Impact: Councils can't see the full population under their authority who claim benefits.  In this case, that makes analysis of social tariff uptake far more uncertain
Evidence:
"Richmond and Wandsworth have data on only 45% of Universal Credit recipients. This is because the DWP only shares data on people who make an active claim for council tax support." (p12)

"Of the 20,895 households in or at risk of arrears, we were able to match 4,373 (20.9%) addresses in the benefits data. The remaining 79.1% of households will be a combination of:1. Households in arrears but not on means tested benefits. Of the approximately 220,000 households in Richmond and Wandsworth, Policy in Practice only has visibility of under 13%.2. Households claiming Universal Credit that are not claiming council tax reduction. Because DWP does not include these households in the Universal Credit Data Share, local authorities do not receive their data and therefore they are not part of the LIFT [Low Income Family Tracker] data. There are over 20,000 households in this category for Richmond and Wandsworth.3. Households that exist in the benefits data but have mismatched addresses in the two
datasets (that cannot be resolved using Policy in Practice’s address matching tool)." (p15)
Report: Automating access to water social tariffs: A pilot study with Thames Water in Richmond and Wandsworth
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: LFS overstates post-pandemic inactivity rise
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: The Labour Force Survey appears to over-state the rise in economic inactivity since 2019.
Questions: Has economic inactivity actually risen since the pandemic, or is this a measurement error?
Impact: Inflated perception of an “inactivity crisis” may misdirect policy resources.
Evidence: “…the LFS currently says that the 16+ inactivity rate has risen by 1 percentage point from Q4 2019 to Q3 2024, but… [admin-based estimate shows] zero change…” (p. 11)
Report: Get Britain’s Stats Working
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: WAS methodology change obscures generational wealth analysis
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: New methods applied to Wealth and Assets Survey leave old data unreliable and obscure analysis of wealth distribution across generations.
Questions: Various wealth-related questions, including about intergenerational wealth distribution
Impact: Old data still unreliable; distribution of wealth across generations is less reliable
Evidence: Furthermore, recently the ONS changed its methodology in its Wealth and Assets Survey and introduced two measures that improved the reliability of the latest version but haven’t corrected it for earlier versions. The new methodology also includes a change on how the value of future pension income is converted into today’s terms, making analysis of the distribution of wealth across generations much less reliable.
Report: Independent Review into National Statistics
Author: Women's Budget Group
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Distributional Analysis); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Wealth & Assets)

Name: Government lacks systematic Pupil Premium spending data
Topics: Misc
Headline: Government lacks systematic, robust data on how Pupil Premium funding is spent
Questions: What are schools spending Pupil Premium on and what impact does that expenditure have on disadvantaged pupils?
Impact: Without comparable spending data, effectiveness cannot be evaluated and funds may be misdirected
Evidence:
“Government lacks a clear picture of how PP is being spent because of a lack of systematic, robust accountability processes.” (p6)

"In the longer term, the DfE should establish a system for systematically collecting
data on how schools spend PP funding. This would enable more rigorous tracking, analysis, and accountability of spending patterns, and support the development of more strategic, impactfocused use of the funding across the school system." (p51)
Report: Unequal Returns: The case for reforming the Pupil Premium
Author: Centre for Social Justice

Name: UC housing element not separately reported
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Housing element of Universal Credit not separately reported in UCFS/HBAI, limiting analysis of housing support.
Questions: What is the impact of the housing element of UC on poverty reduction?
Impact: Prevents evaluation of housing support adequacy or impact; hinders policy design on housing and poverty.
Evidence:
"Families on low-income benefits sometimes get support for their housing costs. In the legacy system, this was through Housing Benefit but in the current system, it is largely through the housing element of UC. Unfortunately, in UC, the housing element is not easily reported as a separate amount. This means that in the
Households Below Average Income data it is not possible to analyse the impact of the housing element of UC." (p62)
Report: Poverty in Scotland 2024
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Secure FRS access severely restricted
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Access to more granular and practically useful versions of FRS data from the UK Data Service is strictly controlled, which can frustrate researchers outside of government getting to use it.
Questions: Various about LHA adequacy
Impact: Either researchers have to use a less granular dataset or go through an extensive and cumbersome security procedure
Evidence:
"The UKDS has recently made available a secure version of the FRS through its secure data services (UK Data Service, n.d.). In the secure version of the FRS, geographical information at a more detailed level, including BRMA, is provided in the dataset. However, there are strict conditions on access to the data.
Researchers have to undergo accredited training on data handling, the research project has to satisfy a public benefit test and must be approved by the data owner, the DWP. Furthermore, users cannot download the data to their own computers: instead, they must use a secure connection to the UKDS servers, carry out analysis remotely on UKDS machines, and all outputs from the analysis (tables, charts, and so on) have to be approved individually by the UKDS to ensure that none of them contain any risk of individuals being identified." (p19)
Report: Stop the freeze: permanently re-link housing benefits to private rents
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: FSM eligibility data inconsistent over time
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Free School Meals (FSM) eligibility data in National Pupil Database have been inconsistent over time, frustrating interpretation of changes and policy impacts over time.
Questions: How consistent, valid and usable is the FSM measure in the National Pupil Database as a proxy for child poverty and for tracking gaps over time?
Impact: Apparent changes in attainment or wellbeing gaps may simply reflect shifting classification, leading to misinterpretation of trends and mis-targeting of education or anti-poverty policies.
Evidence:
“Compounding known issues with patterns of under-recording in the NPD of FSM-entitled children … findings here indicate that the compositions of the FSM vs non-FSM-ascribed groups have changed over time” (p5)

“This is important because … Variation in the magnitude of differences between groups, and ‘closing’ or ‘widening’ of ‘gaps’ may be due to children being categorised differently, rather than to equivalent children having different outcomes and experiences.” (p11)

"These multiple uncertainties and complexities have implications for thevalidity of comparisons over time between the FSM-attributed and non-FSM-attributed groups, and for whether the causes of changes in ‘gaps’over time can be situated at the level of policies or interventions within theeducation system (or elsewhere) targeted at these groups." (p55)
Report: No such thing as a free lunch? Exploring the consistency, validity, and uses of the Free School Meals measure in the National Pupil Database
Author: LSE CASE -  Campbell & Obolenskaya
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Inadequate migrant child poverty data obscures scale
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Inadequate data on poverty experienced by children with migrant parents obscures the scale of the problem
Questions: How many children with migrant parents live in poverty, and how has this changed over time?
Impact: Obscures prevalence of child poverty among migrant families
Evidence: “Beyond the legal and practical barriers discussed above, there are also more profound systemic barriers embedded within the immigration and welfare systems. Inefficiencies in decision-making and inconsistent application of section 17 support are compounded by inadequate data on poverty experienced by children with migrant parents, obscuring the scale of the problem.” (p20)
Report: Hidden Hardships: The immigration system and child poverty
Author: IPPR
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Migrants)

Name: UC data does not record reasons for deductions
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: More than half of UC deductions records shared with LAs have reasons for the deductions labelled as “Unknown”, obscuring information that may be helpful for reducing deductions.
Questions: What types of UC deductions are most common?
Impact: Detailed analysis of the kinds of deductions that are made is not possible with the limited data that DWP provide
Evidence:
“The reason that a deduction is made is not always visible in the Universal Credit data… The most common category of deduction in the data is ‘Unknown deductions’ representing over 50 % of deductions visible in the data…” (p15)

"It is probable that deductions marked as Unknown Deductions are multiple, simultaneous debt deductions which are not separately defined by the DWP within the data provided to councils." (p15)

“Overall, the limited number of types of deductions identified in the dataset suggests that there may be gaps in the DWP's recording and monitoring of the various types of deductions that can be applied to Universal Credit payments.  It is important to note that this is about to change as the DWP expands the types of deductions that will be visible in the datasets.” (p60)
Report: Hidden holes: Deductions and sanctions for people on Universal Credit
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: DWP benefits data insufficient to target social tariffs on water bills
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: DWP benefits-sharing feed to Thames Water only gives Yes/No flag for receipt of means-tested benefits, but this is insufficient for effectively targetting social tariffs.
Questions: Are social tariffs for water reaching the people who are in need of them?
Impact: This paper presents the results of a collaborative project between Policy in Practice, Thames Water, and two local authorities, the latter holding data that can better identify candidates for auto enrolment on social tariffs.  The poor data sharing between DWP and Thames water necessitates this approach, which requires individual councils (and in this case, third-sector orgs) to partner to understand uptake of social tariffs
Evidence:
"Thames Water is increasing the support available to customers in difficulty, primarily through socialtariffs. However, despite extensive outreach efforts, take up of social tariffs has suffered from lowawareness, and customers sometimes struggle to complete an application.Thames Water has a data sharing agreement with DWP to ask who is in receipt of means tested
benefits, such as Universal Credit or Pension Credit. The data only provides a Yes/No indicator of
receipt, which lacks the detail needed to effectively target social tariffs to those most in need." (p2)
Report: Automating access to water social tariffs: A pilot study with Thames Water in Richmond and Wandsworth
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Utilities); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Regulator impact on protected groups poorly understood
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Impact of regulators' policies on people with protected characteristics is not well understood.
Questions: What is the impact of regulators' policies on people with different protected characteristics?
Impact: Lack of transparency and scrutiny of regulations
Evidence: Those subject to the PSED, and indeed those outside it, should be encouraged to use Equality Impact Assessments to focus as much on advancing equality of opportunity as on eliminating discrimination. In order to do this effectively, data on protected characteristics needs to be collected and used in any impact assessment. To date, very little has been published by regulators on the impact of their policies on those with protected characteristics, and it is not clear whether this is because the data isn’t collected or is collected but not used. Our report has found many gaps in data, and understanding the experience of those with protected characteristics should be built into any research principles, from both a qualitative and quantitative perspective. (p10)
Report: The Inequality of Poverty
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Population Groups (Other)

Name: MIS excludes homeowner maintenance costs
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: MIS treatment of housing costs does not capture homeowners’ maintenance costs.
Questions: What are minimum socially acceptable income standards in the UK over time?
Impact: May understate required income for home owners.
Evidence: “One aspect of housing costs that it [Minimum Income Standards] does not fully capture, however, is home maintenance: the MIS model bases this on what the public think that tenants would need to spend as a minimum, while for homeowners it takes mortgage payments into account, but not the cost of maintaining one’s own home.” (p60)
Report: Households living below a Minimum Income Standard: 2008–2023
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: LFS issues limit local labour market comparisons
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Issues with the Labour Force Survey limit confidence in comparisons of local labour markets
Questions: What is the gap between employment rates in high and low employment areas?
Impact: Conclusions cannot be reached with substantial confidence
Evidence:
"In the period since the pandemic, the UK labour market has been in flux, with rising economic inactivity (primarily due to sickness) and a resultant fall in employment compared to before the pandemic. There are some signs that poor national performance may be pushing regional employment gaps in the wrong direction.12 The absolute gap between high (90th percentile) and low (10th percentile) employment areas is at its highest level since 2017, but data issues with the Labour Force Survey mean it’s difficult to know how robust is this finding; what is more, HMRCs real-time PAYE data suggests
that low-employment areas have continued to see strong employee growth." (p8)
Report: Uneven ground: Assessing the state of UK geographic economic inequality facing the new Government
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Distributional Analysis); Geographic Scope (Other); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Cross-governmental administrative data on homelessness is limited
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Limited cross-government administrative data on homelessness hinders a whole-system view
Questions: Variety of questions relating to the homeless population
Impact: Government cannot plan or resource coordinated prevention and support
Evidence: “Civil servants… identified a lack of cross-governmental understanding of homelessness and rough sleeping policy and available data… The data on homelessness and rough sleeping outside of MHCLG was described as fairly limited, hindering the collective ability of government to build a comprehensive picture of the population… There is also a lack of clarity from within other departments on how homelessness and rough sleeping funding is spent and how outcomes from this spending are measured.” (p40-41)
Report: Systems-wide evaluation of homelessness and rough sleeping: preliminary findings
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact; RSM; Cordis Bright
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: Food bank data not linked
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Data about food bank advice services exists but is not well linked.
Questions:
What are the long-term impacts of food bank advice services?

How do food bank advice service users engage with the system?
Impact: Lack of data linkage limits understanding of impacts of food bank advice services affect wellbeing
Evidence: "A considerable amount of data about the food bank advice services is already collected by advisers and Trussell. Consideration should be given to how existing data can be linked to understand and track people’s journeys. Further standardised data could also be collected. This could be collated and analysed at a national level to produce a set of measures for key impacts on financial wellbeing, for example." (p111)
Report: Evaluating money matters advice and support services provided through food banks
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Food Security)

Name: Limited data sharing hinders social tariff auto-enrolment
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Limited data sharing between LAs, DWP, and HMRC data sharing reduce reach of auto-enrolment for social tariffs.
Questions: How can Auto Enrolment in social tariffs for a variety of utilities be improved?
Impact: Restricts scale and effectiveness of auto-enrolment; many eligible households missed.
Evidence:
"Given central government development times, we expect a 2025 pilot programme will have to be run in partnership with local authorities. DWP can still support LAs in 2025 by:
‡ Endorsing data sharing for this purpose as DWP did with Pension Credit uptake in November 2024. Data sharing for this purpose is both legal and possible, but convincing risk-averse legal teams in both local authorities and utilities of this can be a challenge. An endorsement from DWP would slash the time, cost and uncertainty of agreeing data shares. Failing this, working with companies experienced with local authority data sharing to accelerate the process
‡ Sharing data on all UC recipients with local authorities: DWP shares data on only 20-45% of Universal Credit recipients with local authorities, limiting the reach of Auto Enrol to about one-third of the eligible population. Existing legal gateways would support sharing of data on all residents, and DWP should do this through an expanded feed. This would be substantially faster (and cheaper) than an entirely new feed, which will take several years." (p18)

"HMRC does not permit the use of income data for water poverty, despite legal gateways allowing it to do so. Utilities will need to estimate income based on benefit awards. Data held by local authorities does not include non-dependent income nor housing costs for owner-occupiers, so utilities will either need to ignore these criteria or take an estimation / assumption." (p18)
Report: The art of the possible: How data can improve access to social tariffs in the short and medium term
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Geographic Scope (UK); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Utilities); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: The impact of the FRS Transformation project on broader poverty data remains unclear
Topics: Analysis & Insights
Headline: FRS Transformation experimental data seems to fix issue of missing £40bn of benefit income from regular FRS, but DWP have not yet set out how these changes affect our understanding of income poverty, and inequality and deprivation statistics.
Questions: How much benefit income do households actually receive?
Impact: Experimental stats fix old problem, but have not been integrated into a broader understanding of poverty yet
Update: Updated FRS data reflecting the use of administrative data to replace survey questions were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence:
"The DWP’s report shows that around £40 billion a year of benefit income does not appear in the survey data (as we set out in 2018). DWP has been working hard on fixing this by linking survey responses to real benefit data for each household so that survey errors – such as mis-estimating benefit amounts or forgetting about particular benefits – can be corrected. Doing so would add in around £20 billion of benefit income to 2022-23’s survey data....

But what about the other £20 billion? Well, the new release reveals for the first time that there is also “an underrepresentation of benefit recipients in the FRS sample”.... The DWP sets out how this can be addressed by ensuring that survey weights match not only population figures but also benefit caseloads and employment numbers.  The chart below shows the impacts of these two big changes – correcting survey responses using administrative data and revising population weights – which together would essentially resolve the problem of the missing £40 billion.

This is, however, just an experimental release. DWP has not set out how these changes would affect income, poverty, inequality and deprivation statistics. And it is not straightforward to guess at the impacts because, although correcting under-estimated benefit receipt will boost incomes, correcting the under-representation of the benefit-receiving population essentially means adding more poor households to the data."
Report: Five takeaways from new living standards data
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement)

Name: MIS overlooks disability extra costs
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: The Minimum Income Standard (MIS) overlooks the extra costs of disability (as do official income statistics), so poverty calculations understate the needs of disabled people.
Questions: What are the additional living costs associated with different disabilities?
Impact: Under-estimates the income required for disabled households, which will lead to inaccurate measures of who falls below the threshold
Evidence: While the MIS is a comprehensive measureof acceptable living costs for the majority ofhouseholds, it does not reflect the additional costsof living with a disability. Measuring such costs isdifficult. Each disability is unique in its impact ona person’s life and therefore the additional costwill differ between any two disabilities. In 2015,however, the CRSP28 found that the additional costsfor a single deaf person were 82% higher (£706per month) than the default MIS budget. For thosewith hearing loss, the MIS was 25% higher (£217per month). (p12)
Report: The National Living Income: Guaranteeing a Decent Minimum Income for All
Author: New Economics Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Disability); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Detailed data on modular housing schemes is unavailable
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: A variety of data about modular housing schemes is unavailable, limiting usefulness for understanding these schemes as a possible tool to combat homelessness
Questions: What are the features of various modular housing schemes in the UK?
Impact: Makes it challenging to assess modular housing schemes' effectiveness for homelessness alleviation.  Policy evaluation becomes challenging in the absence of useful data about the intervention.
Evidence:
"For 11 schemes, the unit size was not available." (p9)

“For 24 (73 %) of the schemes, information about the nature of the support provided to residents is not available.” (p9)

“Information about the onsite facilities… is currently limited and needs further research.” (p37)
