“We do not currently have information about the nature of the location of the identified schemes… Further research is needed to explore the nature of the scheme locations and the impacts on residents.” (p 47)
Report: Insights into the use of modular housing in addressing homelessness
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: UC claims data on Stat-Xplore limited and outdated
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: UC claims data in Stat-Xplore are very limited and data migration issues disrupt ability to easily estimate overall caseload
Questions: Could better value for money be achieved by redirecting money currently spent on Housing Benefit and UC?
Impact: Analysts must rely on an old 2013 HB snapshot.
Evidence: “The data available on universal credit claims via DWP stat-xplore is currently very limited … because of the gradual ongoing migration of claims from HB to UC, using the current caseload will seriously distort the caseload mix … the only option was to use the last complete dataset for HB … April 2013.” (p24)
Report: Housing for people on low incomes – how do we make the best use of government subsidies in England?
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Flagship homelessness programmes lack outcome metrics
Topics: Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: There is an absence of relevant outcome & impact metrics for flagship homelessness interventions.
Questions: Are the Homelessness Prevention Grant, RSAP and RSI achieving their intended outcomes?
Impact: Impossible to judge value for money or to learn which approaches reduce homelessness
Evidence:
"Local authorities regularly monitor and report rough sleeping and
homelessness metrics to MHCLG via tools such as the H-CLIC
performance dashboard. However, there is a lack of clear
alignment between these metrics and the programme activities. It was noted
from the evaluation that the focus of the metrics was on outputs. There
appears to be a need to address the lack of outcome and impact data
against the programme terms and conditions. The absence of this is
another factor which makes it difficult to assess whether the programmes
are successfully delivering the intended activities and whether they are
having the intended impact" (p79)
Report: Systems-wide evaluation of homelessness and rough sleeping: preliminary findings
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact; RSM; Cordis Bright
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: National poverty premium measure doesn't exist
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: CMA commissioned a feasibility study about a national poverty premium measure, but such a measure has not yet been produced.
Questions: How large is the poverty premium at the national level?
Impact: Poverty premium is not measured at national level or with the highest-quality data
Evidence: Notably,there has been an interest in the production of a measure to track the overallpoverty premium at a national level. The Competition and Markets Authoritycommissioned a feasibility study to investigate the measurement of thepoverty premium in the UK.24 There has also been interest in measuring otherareas where often vulnerable customers are disadvantaged: for example, themethodology was adopted by Citizen Advice, to calculate a mental healthpoverty premium.25 However, to date, no other measurement of the UKpoverty premium has been produced. (p15)
Report: The poverty premium in 2022
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (UK); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Scale of 'hidden homelessness' is unknown
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: True scale of hidden homelessness is not estimable with existing data.
Questions: What is the true scale, distribution and demographic profile of hidden homelessness in the UK?
Impact: Stunts any quantitative research into hidden homelessness
Evidence:
“The ONS has stated that it is not currently possible to estimate the true scale of hidden homelessness…” (p172)

“Whilst the overall number of people hidden homeless is unavailable, charities told the Commission that it was an issue they regularly came across in their work.” (p173)
Report: Two Nations: The State of Poverty in the UK
Author: Centre for Social Justice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: SIC Codes do not reflect jobs typically held by women
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope
Headline: SIC Codes reflect outdated picture of UK economy, leading to lack of nuance in understanding of jobs typically held by women.
Questions: Various relating to gender breakdowns of job sectors
Impact: Lack of nuance in understanding job roles that are overrepresentative of women
Evidence:
"SIC Codes and Caring Professions – The Standard Industrial Classification (SIC)
system can obscure the nature of women’s employment, particularly in care roles where they are overrepresented. An analysis of the classification shows that it provides a more detailed breakdown of traditionally male dominated roles compared to traditionally female dominated roles - a consequence of its reflection of the job market when it was first created. The classification fails to reflect evolving roles and seniority levels in women dominated fields, such as in nursing where different types of nurses are not easily distinguished, making detailed employment analysis difficult." (p2)
Report: Gender, data and the labour market:Why data collection is not gender neutral
Author: Women's Budget Group
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (UK); Population Groups (Women & Gender); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Yearly EU-SILC material deprivation measure is at household level only
Topics: Poverty Characteristics; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Standard EU-SILC material deprivation indicator only covers household level. Adapted indicators for individuals and children collected every 3 years, not annually.
Questions: Measuring individual and child-level material deprivation
Impact: Researchers cannot test how bargaining power, gender or age affect individual poverty risk inside the same household.
Evidence:
"Though useful for measuring and monitoring material deprivation across
the EU Member States, the official material deprivation indicator had one
major shortcoming: the deprivation items that are included in the index are
collected at household level (i.e., only one household member provides
information) and the deprivation status is then assigned to all household
members. Thus, it cannot be used to infer inequality in deprivation
outcomes across household members (including children)..." (p11)

NB: The authors go on to say that the EU adopted an individual-level deprivation indicator in 2018, and there is a child version of this with a few problems as noted below.  This will be collected going forward every 3 years.
Report: Living arrangements, intra-household inequality and children’s deprivation: Evidence from EU-SILC
Author: LSE CASE - (Burchardt & Karagiannaki)
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intra-Household Inequality); Population Groups (Women & Gender); Poverty Characteristics (Material Deprivation)

Name: Small Welsh samples limit ethnicity-based poverty analysis
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Small sample sizes in HBAI data for Wales make analysis of poverty by ethnic group less confident, even with averaging over many years.
Questions: What are the poverty rates for different ethnic groups?
Impact: Limits confidence in ethnicity-based analysis of poverty
Evidence:
"Figure 11 shows a time series of poverty rates by ethnic group, which uses
a 7-year average to overcome small sample sizes. Nevertheless, the smaller
sample size means that variations in who is surveyed each year can have
a more pronounced effect on annual values compared to the large white
population. This makes it difficult to determine whether these changes
over time truly reflect the experiences of people from ethnic minority
groups, and if they are linked to shifts in the factors driving poverty for
them, if they are due to structural changes, such as new policies, or if they
are driven by volatility in the data. Nonetheless, the data over the past
20 years shows that the white population of Wales have consistently had
lower rates of poverty compared to the ethnic minority population." (p27)
Report: Poverty in Wales 2025
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Modular housing outcome data missing
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Outcomes for residents of modular housing schemes are missing
Questions: What short- and long-term outcomes (stability, health, employment) do residents experience?
Impact: Cannot assess whether schemes improve outcomes
Evidence:
"Generally, there is currently a lack of robust evidence to evaluate the
projects and the outcomes for residents, making it difficult to compare
them with more traditional forms of accommodation. For example,
although it is generally considered that modular and container housing
is less costly than other forms of temporary accommodation, there
is little robust cost data available to determine the exact cost of
building modular and container units." (p51)
Report: Insights into the use of modular housing in addressing homelessness
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: UC admin data lack intersectional demographic detail
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Limited demographic and intersectional data – e.g., disability by ethnicity – restricts nuanced understanding of Universal Credit recipient population and their experiences.
Questions: Are particular ethnic or vulnerable groups more likely to face delays or sanctions in UC?
Impact: Intersectionality & Subgroups not always possible. Unable to monitor fairness of welfare delivery or address systemic bias
Evidence:
"There is a significant ethnicity data gap in DWP Universal Credit data and government communications were not always well targeted." (p10)
“The National Audit Office highlighted that DWP did not collect data on specific claimant vulnerabilities, and only had insufficient ethnicity data, so meaningful analysis cannot be undertaken…” (p 55)

“The evidence base is more complete… for some characteristic-based intersections (such as age by sex), but less complete for others (such as disability by ethnicity), indicating a need for more in-depth research…” (p 76)
Report: The financial impact of the pandemic
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Housing datasets lack linkage identifiers
Topics: Essential Services
Headline: Some housing datasets do not systematically collect personally identifiable data that would allow linkage to other admin datasets
Questions: Is it possible to link data from a variety of services related to homelessness?
Impact: Linkage between some datasets is not possible or is not high quality
Evidence:
"Where data on personal identifiers (such as name, date of birth, or health or social securitynumbers) are incompletely or incorrectly recorded, individuals may be missed from the linkage
process, potentially skewing results. For example, 25% of records from the City and County of
Swansea housing team data used in the three research case studies could not be assigned to a
unique person and were not linkable, compared to less than 5% from the SMDS, where personal
details are a requirement of the collection." (p8)
Report: What Works: Research Impact and Homelessness – Houses, Not Homelessness
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: FRS data have multi-year time lag
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: FRS data suffer from multi-year time lag
Questions: What is the level of poverty in the UK?
Impact: Researchers can't get a real-time update on poverty trends, which makes responsive and timely policymaking a challenge.
Evidence:
"In 2022-23 (currently the most recent year
covered by official data), 4.3 million children in the UK (30 per cent) were growing up
in relative poverty after housing costs, considerably higher than the 16 per cent of
pensioners and 18 per cent of working-age non-parents who had a low household income
on this metric (see Figure 1)." (p7)
Report: Turning the tide: What it will take to reduce child poverty in the UK
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Disability benefits counted as income, costs ignored
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Disability benefits count as income in HBAI, but additional costs associated with disability are not considered.
Questions: What percent of people with disabilities are in poabout
Impact: Poverty rates for those with disabilities will be under-stated
Evidence: 26 per cent of households where someone is disabled were in relative low income AHC in 2018/19. The House of Commons Library briefing (Frances-Devine 2020) notes that, while disability benefits are included as income in the HBAI, the data does not take into consideration the additional living costs incurred by people with disabilities (p18)
Report: The Inequality of Poverty
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Disability); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Devolved education systems prevent UK-wide comparison
Topics: Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Devolved nature of education systems makes comparison of attainment between UK nations impossible.
Questions: What is the educational attainment gap between children in and out of poverty?
Impact: Comparison between nations is not possible in a direct way
Evidence:
"Education is a devolved power, which means that education systems vary
across the 4 countries in the UK. Attainment in Wales is therefore not
directly comparable to any of the other UK nations. In most cases, schools
and governments do not collect information on the incomes of parents, so
we need to use other information that is available to identify when children
might be growing up in a household in poverty or with low/lower incomes." (p70)
Report: Poverty in Wales 2025
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Education); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Scottish LA homelessness spending opaque
Topics: Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Scottish local authorities do not publish transparent accounts of how much they spend on homelessness services, which makes accountability and policy evaluation harder
Questions: How much do councils spend on homelessness prevention and response?
Impact: Hinders evaluation of adequacy of homelessness services
Evidence:
"Like education, homelessness funding is an often-opaque area with the majority of
it delivered through the local government general revenue grant – and so, similar
to education, it is not possible to determine precise spend at a local authority area
where funding isn’t ring-fenced. However, we can again use the local government
finance green book to look at funding assumptions and provide a guide (table 5.1)." (p44)
Report: Tipping the Scales: The social and economic harm of poverty in Scotland
Author: IPPR
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority)

Name: Economic statistics ignore broader wellbeing measures
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Economic statistics take a narrow view of wellbeing
Questions: What does economic wellbeing look like in the UK holistically?
Impact: Our understanding of wellbeing lacks comprehensiveness
Evidence:
"Understanding wellbeing – Standard economic metrics focus on employment and
income but fail to account for broader wellbeing measures, such as unpaid care burdens
and job insecurity, which disproportionately impact women." (p3)
Report: Gender, data and the labour market:Why data collection is not gender neutral
Author: Women's Budget Group
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Population Groups (Women & Gender)

Name: FRS data cannot distinguish between benefit under-reporting and non-uptake or non-receipt of benefits
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Benefits?receipt is under?reported in the FRS, which muddies assessment of why households in deep poverty receive no social security support.
Questions: What share of children in very deep poverty receive benefits?
Impact: If benefits receipt was accurately accounted for in FRS, it would be easier to assess how much of the lack of support to households in deep poverty is because of poor uptake among the eligible.
Update: DWP have been working on using benefits administrative data to replace survey questions about benefits.  Updated datasets since 2018/19 were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence: It is unclear why just under 3 in 10 children in very deep poverty and, in turn, who are living in households with very low incomes, receive little to no support through social security. This could in part be due to known issues with benefits reporting in the Family Resources Survey, but households not claiming benefits to which they are entitled is very likely to also play a role.
Report: Children being left behind: deep poverty among families in Scotland
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Population Groups (Children & Families); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Current data infrastructure does not support rapid, universal cash payments
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Current data infrastructure does not allow for rapid, universal cash support payments to all households in the country
Questions: How can government social security policies be quicker and more responsive in the face of newly emergent needs?
Impact: Policies to fight emergency economic distress are slow, inefficient, and poorly targeted.
Evidence: The new system [of one-off cost-of-living payments] would establish the first means for the government to pay cash support to all households in the country, which could be used as a powerful means of welfare support in future. National insurance numbers would also allow the government to link households with their HMRC data, giving the option to target future payments by household income if needed (outside of means-tested benefits, the government can currently only target support by individual income, which could lead to over-supporting or under-supporting at the household level). (p16)
Report: Warm Homes, Cool Planet
Author: New Economics Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Energy Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Accessibility of Census data tools could be improved for non-technical data users
Topics: Population Groups; Geographic Scope
Headline: Census data are not always user-friendly or easily accessible for non-technical users.
Questions: Various
Impact: Some users may be excluded from accessing the data
Evidence: The ONS should continue to develop its accessibility tools by adding the option to present downloaded census data in a pre-made table format similar to its NOMIS platform. The list format in which data is currently presented requires an understanding of how to analyse and interpret data through the use of formulae and/or tools such as Excel PivotTables. The option to include rates (percentages) alongside observations would further improve accessibility by removing the need for an understanding of how to do this within Excel. Finally, functionality could be improved by enabling the data to be examined across different area types (e.g., Local Authorities and regions)within the same spreadsheet. This would encourage more assessment of absolute as well as relative gender inequalities.
Report: The Census and Feminism: How Census 2021 Data Benefits Feminist Analysis and Activism
Author: Women’s Budget Group & Global Institute for Women’s Leadership
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Population Groups (Women & Gender)

Name: Statutory homelessness stats are collected differently in each devolved nation and also undercount disabled homeless population
Topics: Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Published statutory statistics only include disability data for households found to be owed a homelessness duty by their local council, even though this does not cover all people experiencing homelessness.
Questions: What is the true scale of homelessness among people with disabilities across the UK, given inconsistent statutory recording?How do differing definitions and practices shape the evidence base?
Impact: Under-recognition of the scale of disabled homelessness
Evidence:
"Differences in the way homelessness is assessed affect how the
number of people with disabilities experiencing homelessness is
recorded. It is a legal requirement that local authorities collect and
report data on people owed a homelessness duty. This is collected via
the Homelessness Case Level Collection (H-CLIC) in England and the
Access Homelessness Monitoring System (HL1) in Scotland. Statistics
on homelessness for Northern Ireland are sourced from the NIHE, and
in Wales data are collected by local housing authorities via quarterly
statistical returns and published on StatsWales. Additional annual
homelessness data is published on the Welsh Government website. Excluding Northern Ireland, published statutory statistics only
include disability data for households which were found to be owed
a homelessness duty by their local council. People with disabilities
applying for homelessness support may be referred to social services and there are no national guidelines regarding how priority need
and vulnerability are assessed. It is therefore likely that statutory
homelessness statistics underestimate the number of people with
disabilities experiencing homelessness across the UK." (p14-15)
Report: Homelessness and Disability in the UK
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Population Groups (Disability)

Name: Uninsurance data missing, distorting poverty premium
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Data on uninsurance rates are hard to get, and uninsurance is not reflected in poverty premiums.
Questions: How do protected characteristics intersect to influence exposure to poverty premiums?
Impact: Lack of uninsurance data potentially distorts interpretation of poverty premium estimates
Evidence: However, based on the survey data, the risk of incurring insurance-based poverty premiums seems to be lower among people with protected characteristics, because people from BAME groups, lone parents, and people with disabilities, particularly those with mental health issues, were less likely to hold any insurance. Being uninsured may of course lead to worse detriment than paying over the odds. This ‘going without’ is often the flip side of paying a poverty premium (Davies et al, 2016). It can signify a level of poverty or exclusion from the market that bars participation in even basic services and goods. This deprivation is not reflected in increased poverty premiums but needs to be accounted for. As it is difficult to measure, it is easily overlooked, but should be recognized as a feature of poverty as tangible as the poverty premium itself. (p7)
Report: The Inequality of Poverty
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Poverty Characteristics (Uninsurance); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Siloed systems hinder early homelessness identification
Topics: Misc
Headline: Siloed data systems and incompatible monitoring hinder early identification of people at risk of homelessness.
Questions: How can improved data sharing and architecture enable earlier intervention in homelessness?
Impact: Prevents early identification of at-risk individuals and timely preventative support.
Evidence: “The government should invest in improved data sharing and architecture… systemic barriers, including a siloed working culture and incompatible or, at least, poorly aligned data-collection and monitoring systems across government, significantly hinder opportunities to identify people who are at risk of homelessness much earlier.” (pp. 31-32)
Report: A smarter approach to homelessness
Author: Institute for Government & Centre for Homelessness Impact

Name: FRS lacks religion data and ethnicity samples are small
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Missing religion data and small ethnicity samples restrict Intersectionality & Subgroups
Questions: How do poverty risks vary by religion and ethnicity among larger families?
Impact: Breakdown of poverty trends for religious groups is not possible
Evidence: “The FRS does not collect data on religion, but it does include data on ethnicity, allowing us to construct a consistent time-series back to 2001/02, although sample sizes are small.” (p 15)
Report:
A time of need: Exploring the changing
poverty risk facing larger families in
the UK
Author: LSE CASE - Stewart et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities)

Name: Small Scotland samples in FRS force seven-year data pooling
Topics: Poverty Characteristics; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Small Scotland/sub?group samples force pooling across a very wide time window (7 years)
Questions: How many children are in very deep poverty in Scotland?
Impact: Analysis over time becomes very crude and not sensitive to active changes in the policy environment
Evidence: In the most recent data (2016–23 – we move to a 7-year average in the remainder of this briefing to avoid a small sample size and increased volatility), people in households with children in very deep poverty are, on average, 54% below the poverty line. This means that their incomes are less than half of what is needed to reach the poverty line. Just over 3 in 10 children in poverty are in households in very deep poverty.
Report: Children being left behind: deep poverty among families in Scotland
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Population Groups (Children & Families); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty)

Name: Lack of individual-level homelessness intervention engagement data
Topics: Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of individual-level data on engagement with homelessness interventions limits ability to accurately measure impact of intervention.
Questions: Are a variety of homelessness interventions effective at tackling homelessness?
Impact: Impact evaluation suffers since treatment cannot be precisely attributed to individuals
Evidence:
"We see no significant impacts of the three interventions being evaluated on our primary
outcome — whether a young care leaver is identified as homeless by the local authority. Although this is disheartening, we must consider the shortcomings of our analysis.Specifically, by using local authority-level dosage data, and individual-level outcomes
data, we are unable to attribute a specific individual’s treatment to that individualexperiencing homelessness, or ceasing to. This means that our analysis is broadly anintention to treat at the level of the local authority/year, and we are in effect evaluatingthe impact of making heavier use of the intervention, compared to the past and
compared to peer local authorities. This type of analysis, although not expected to be
biased, is very likely to be attenuated, with any impact of the intervention on outcomesfor a treated individual “watered down” by the outcomes for care leavers in the samelocal authority who did not receive the intervention.
Perhaps most importantly for our interpretation, two of the interventions —Housing
First and A Bed Every Night — aim to reduce rough sleeping by placing people into
temporary or emergency accommodation. Although these might have downstream
effects on homelessness per se, the main impacts are likely to shift people between
different types of homelessness, which are not differentiated in our data." (p34)
Report: Investigating the Impacts of Housing Interventions on Homelessness among Care Leavers in Greater Manchester
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact (CHI) & Greater Manchester Combined Authority

Name: Bottom 5% excluded from analysis, data unreliable
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Researchers exclude bottom 5% of income distribution from analysis due to HBAI data quality concerns
Questions: Measuring incomes for the very poorest households?
Impact: Poorest households not properly analysable
Update: DWP have been working on using benefits administrative data to replace survey questions about benefits.  Updated datasets since 2018/19 were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence: “We exclude the bottom 5 per cent, due to concerns about the reliability of data for this group.” (p42)  Comes in the context of looking at income trends for various groups.
Report: The Living Standards Audit 2022
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Distributional Analysis); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Home Office asylum data severely limited
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Home Office publishes very limited disaggregated information on children in asylum-seeking families or about Asylum Support claims, leaving major information gaps about who they are and what support they need.
Questions: How many children are in families receiving Asylum Support and what are their characteristics (age, disability, family structure, etc)?
Impact: A basic understanding of asylum-seeking children and their families cannot be obtained
Evidence:
"[Children's] ‘invisibility’ is also apparent in the lack of basic demographic and
disaggregated data available to inform policymaking, research, and service
development.... Yet beyond this, there is
very limited publicly available, disaggregated information about children
applying for asylum in the UK with their families. Aside from a basic count
and some demographic information on children’s sex, nationality, and
broad age categories, there is no data on children’s ethnicity, race or
religion, or those of their parents. Nor is there any publicly available data
on family structure and size, the precise age ranges of children, or whether
they or their parents have a disability. The publicly available data tends to
categorise applicants as either ‘main applicants’ or ‘dependents’, so while
it may be possible to obtain some age-disaggregated data, it is not possible
to know the relationship between adults and children within family groups,
such as whether children are claiming asylum with parents, older siblings,
grandparents, or other carers." (p1)
Report: Unseen Children: The hidden lives of children in families seeking asylum in the UK
Author: Ilona Pinter, Centre for Analysis of Social Exclusion (LSE)
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Migrants)

Name: Census 2021 omits crucial information on gendered economic inequality
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Census 2021 omits key variables, including income, earnings, and social security, but also parenting/childcare and experiences of violence.
Questions: Various
Impact: Census 2021 is not as useful as other data sources for feminist economic analysis, and the potential strengths of the Census can't be leveraged for understanding questions of interest to feminist researchers.  See quoted Recommendation relating to this gap.
Evidence: The exclusion of key topic areas limits the census as a tool for illuminating gender inequality. For example, no data is collected regarding incomes, earnings or social security, all of which are crucial for developing an understanding of women’s economic inequality. There is also no data measuring time spent caring for dependent children (which is not included in the census classification of ‘unpaid care’) or violence against women and girls, which are both central feminist issues. (p8)
Report: The Census and Feminism: How Census 2021 Data Benefits Feminist Analysis and Activism
Author: Women’s Budget Group & Global Institute for Women’s Leadership
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intra-Household Inequality); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Women & Gender); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Vendor systems constrain LWA data capture
Topics: Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Vendor systems and local coding practices constrain what LWA data can be captured.
Questions: How can councils consistently evaluate LWA effectiveness and compare schemes?
Impact: Fragmentation and inconsistent coding reduce comparability and completeness.
Evidence: Data currently held by councils has limitations for LWA scheme evaluation purposes. LWA administration data supplied to Policy in Practice for this project shows significant variation between councils. Councils typically use LWA software provided by one of three suppliers, Civica, Capita, or Northgate, and the range of data that can be collected is partially determined by the software supplier. Software suppliers are usually willing to add additional fields but any council specific additions to the standard software can prove expensive for councils. Across councils using the same software there is also variation as councils set borough-specific codes and determine their own use of data fields.  (p19)
Report: Evaluation of Local Welfare Assistance: Final framework and research findings
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority)

Name: Information on income and energy bills is not integrated
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of integrated information about income and energy bills limits the effectiveness of emergency policy measures.
Questions: How can winter fuel payments be most effectively targetted towards people who need them?
Impact: Policy cannot be targeted as effectively or efficiently as it could, which limits government's ability to respond to crises.
Evidence:
“Designing a solution that meets all of these criteria is decidedly tricky. Our most obvious “tool” is the benefit system, but that confronts take-up problems, and lacks information on households’ energy needs. The alternative mechanism – acting more directly on energy bills – can easily pick out high costs, but is unable to pinpoint who has a low income. ” (p. 9)

“Sadly, tardy progress on required data matching within Government … means that it is simply too late to have a comprehensive social tariff up and running this winter.” (p. 12)

“All potential quick fixes are imperfect … there is not yet a good and systematic way to target those with low incomes and high fuel bills.” (p. 12)
Report: Cold Comfort: Mitigating the Winter Fuel Payment cut
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Energy Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Mental health definition inconsistent across NHS trusts
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of shared definition of mental health across NHS trusts may may limit comparability of data across geographies
Questions: What is the prevalence of mental health issues in the UK?
Impact: Inconsistent definitions mean mental-health statistics cannot be compared across areas or tracked over time, masking the true scale of mental-health-driven economic inactivity and poverty.
Evidence: "There is a real growth in mental health problems in the UK.... Yet incredibly NHS Mental Health Trusts do not have a universally understood definition of mental health: only three of the 49 trusts define mental health at all." (pg 9)
Report: How to Get Britain Working (and Pay the Bills)
Author: Centre for Social Justice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Low FRS/HBAI sample sizes limit subgroup poverty analysis
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Low FRS/HBAI sample size for some groups of children reduces statistical power of analysis of poverty among subgroups.
Questions: How did child poverty rates change for different groups of children during the first part of the 21st century?
Impact: Statistical power is reduced for various subgroups of children, making detection of significant changes over time or between groups harder
Evidence: “One key issue relates to sample size. As is wellknown, FRS/HBAI sample size is low for some groups of children. For thepurposes of the SPDO child poverty research exercise, in order to makebest use of the available data, we planned at the outset to base our mainestimates for some breakdowns (ethnicity, region and young carer status)using a pooled data approach. In addition, as the analysis progressed, inundertaking the descriptive analysis, we carefully followed DWPrecommendations in using new bespoke resamples datasets that estimateuncertainty around our child poverty estimates. The main advantage of thisapproach is that the FRS complex survey design can be accounted for whenstatistical significance is assessed. However, one key issue we encounteredis that the resamples datasets contains only around half the number ofunique households of the original sample. Consequently, sample size forchildren is further reduced and this is a particular concern for the smallerbreakdowns that are the specific focus of this paper and this has potentialimplications for statistical power and the assessment of statisticalsignificance.... However, looking forward, we believe that to address these issues of sample size, it might ultimately be necessary to increase sample size in the FRS survey to support the analysis of trends in child poverty for smaller social groups more adequately.” (p6-7)
Report: Going backwards? The slowdown, stalling and reversal of progress in reducing child poverty in Britain during the second decade of the 21st century
Author: LSE CASE – Vizard et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Poor data sharing keeps services siloed
Topics: Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Poor data-sharing means services remain siloed
Questions: How can services better work together to improve the lives of children in local areas?
Impact: Services remain disjointed and efforts may be duplicated
Evidence:
“Reform should pay strong attention to ease of access, and should improve the interoperability of services by improving data sharing. Local authorities should work with the NHS to establish partnerships and joint commissioning of services around a set of shared outcomes for the local community. By partnering with community organisations, they should aim to close gaps between services, improve outreach to identify children who are ‘invisible’, and build trust in support services.” (p19)

"Many centres and hubs cannot access the data they require
to understand and meet local needs, and do not know how many
children or families live in the areas they serve. This makes outreach
more challenging, and some children and parents miss out on support
at a crucial time in their lives. The methods currently used to collect
data are often patchy, unreliable and financially inefficient.76 Some
centres have formal arrangements with other local agencies to
access this data, but others do not." (p42)
Report: Early Years Commission: A Cross-Party Manifesto
Author: Centre for Social Justice, Fabian Society
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: LFS, FRS, ASHE suffer declining response rates
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: LFS and other datasets suffer from declining response rates, volatility, and gaps in capturing workforce trends.
Questions: Various labour-market related questions
Impact: Secondary employment is undercounted in major surveys
Evidence: Recent scrutiny of the Labour Force Survey (LFS) has highlighted major issues in labour market data collection, including declining response rates, volatility and gaps in capturing workforce trends. These challenges are not unique to the LFS. Similar issues affect other important datasets, such as the Family Resources Survey (FRS) and the Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings (ASHE).
Report: Independent Review into National Statistics
Author: Women's Budget Group
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Poorest 5% excluded from FRS distributional analysis
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Headline: The poorest 5 % of households are excluded from FRS distributional analysis because of unreliable data
Questions: What would be the distributional impacts of replacing UC with a National Living Income?
Impact: Very bottom of the income distribution not accounted for in analysis of theoretical change in policy
Evidence: “The first vingtile of households, i.e. the poorest 5 % of households in the FRS are dropped from distributional analysis due to inconsistent data in the FRS but are retained in total costings.” (p25)
Report: The National Living Income: Guaranteeing a Decent Minimum Income for All
Author: New Economics Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Distributional Analysis); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Income-based poverty measures struggle to account for differential experiences of inflation across the income distribution without actual costs data
Topics: Economic Resources
Headline: Income-based poverty measures as found in HBAI / FRS are not complemented by sufficient costs data to account for rapid and heterogeneous inflation across the income distribution, which led researchers to rely on deprivation metrics instead.
Questions: How well do real-income trends capture people's experience when inflation differs across income groups?
Impact: Comparative analysis of trends in real incomes become somewhat less reliable during periods of high, heterogeneous inflation. Alternative metrics (more crude deprivation indicators) were used as a way around the lack of cost data.
Evidence: “Looking at a measure of material deprivation is particularly important in recent years, as measures of real household incomes, by construction, assume that all people face the same inflation rates. This has not been true during the cost-of-living crisis as higher gas, electricity and food prices have disproportionately affected lower-income people.” (p 16)
Report: How have pensioner incomes and poverty changed in recent years?
Author: IFS
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Student homelessness rates unknown
Topics: Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: No systematic data on the rate of student homelessness.
Questions: What is the prevalence and what are the risk factors for homelessness among UK students?
Impact: Universities cannot target prevention support for at-risk students without basic prevalence data
Evidence:
“They could start by collecting data on the housing stability of their students… Rates and instances of homelessness among students in the UK should be the subject of further robust research.” (p8)

"Some of this information may be held by universities on their student records management system; but how many would really know, and indeed follow up, if an undergraduate was evicted from or left private rented housing to sleep on a course mate’s sofa? ... Adding one simple question on housing stability to anonymised surveys of their current students would fill a gap in this data." (p29)
Report: Could Universities Do More to End Homelessness?
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Population Groups (Other)

Name: HBAI samples too small for insights into ethnic sub-groups
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: HBAI sample sizes are too small to reveal differences in child poverty between specific Black ethnic sub-groups, even after grouping multiple years of data
Questions: How does child poverty vary across detailed ethnic sub-groups, and how are those inequalities changing over time?
Impact: Important poverty trends remain invisible
Evidence: “Unfortunately, one of the key difficulties with understanding the nuances in child poverty by ethnicity is that we do not gather enough data for a detailed or up-to-date analysis of the issue. For instance, the survey we use to estimate poverty in the UK does not sample enough Black and minority ethnic families to produce poverty estimates with just a single year of data so we have to average the data over three years. Even then, the sample is not large enough to break down people with a ‘Black ethnicity’ into smaller groups. This is especially important as there are distinct differences between Black Caribbean and Black African communities that are masked by this aggregation.” (p. 3)
Report: Inequalities amplified – The alarming rise of child poverty in the UK
Author: Child Poverty Action Group (CPAG), Women's Budget Group, and Runnymede Trust
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: NOLB lacks outcomes measurement framework
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: No One Left Behind scheme in Scotland lacks a measurement framework or data collection to track employment outcomes associated with its intervention
Questions: Are NOLB interventions successful at generating positive employment outcomes for those in poverty?
Impact: Evaluation of a policy will not be possible in a robust way
Evidence:
"In any system that is seeking to support people with a range of needs and at
differing levels of readiness for the labour market, parking and creaming must
be avoided or you risk pushing people even further away. However, that does
not mean that transparency and accountability of funding should not exist.
Indeed, while an explicit purpose of NOLB was to judge success by more than
job outcomes, there is no clear measurement framework, or data collection/
publication, to evidence what those outcomes are.
While the government provides grant funding to each local authority for NOLB –
and this will be supplemented by funding for previously FSS-delivered services
– it is not clear on what basis these grant amounts are arrived at or what is
expected in return." (p32)
Report: The Role of Employability in Tackling Poverty
Author: IPPR

Questions: How have levels of rough sleeping changed over time and across regions in the UK?
Impact: Research relies on snapshot surveys that don't give a comprehensive picture of rough sleeping

Questions: What does deprivation look like for seats held by different political parties?
Impact: Welsh constituencies excluded from analysis
