Name: Temporary accommodation cases invisible in Stat-Xplore Housing Benefits data
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services
Headline: Temporary-accommodation cases cannot be isolated in Stat-Xplore HB data
Questions: Could better value for money be achieved by redirecting money currently spent on Housing Benefit and UC?
Impact: Data are likely to be inaccessible without a lot of effort and/or technical expertise, or otherwise without being able to get access via a more restricted route.
Evidence: “Apart from the actual spend figures it is not possible to identify temporary accommodation (TA) cases from the DWP’s stat-xplore database. Therefore, they have to be estimated using a variety of other sources. The DWP provides a breakdown of HB expenditure which shows the total annual TA spend but not the caseload.” (p24)
Report: Housing for people on low incomes – how do we make the best use of government subsidies in England?
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Local authorities do not receive comprehensive data on UC claimants
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Universal Credit Data Share (UCDS) admin dataset does not cover all households receiving UC, as not all of them have CT liability or are eligible for CT reduction.
Questions: What is the distribution and impact of debt deductions and sanctions on benefits recipients?
Impact: Analysis of the impact of UC deductions and sanctions will miss the portion of the population that are not eligible for CT support and those not liable for CT.
Evidence: This dataset is held by local authorities in the administration of their Local Council Tax Support Scheme for working age households who apply for Universal Credit. It includes demographic information on the make up of a household, information on earned and unearned income, as well as details of Universal Credit awards. However, this dataset does not cover all households receiving Universal Credit within a local authority. It covers a subset of households who are receiving Universal Credit with either a corresponding Council Tax Support award or who have expressed an interest in being considered for a Council Tax Support claim. Not all households receiving Universal Credit have a council tax liability or are eligible for council tax reduction, and data is not available on these households. The share of households receiving UC that also receive council tax reduction varies by local authority, ranging from 25 - 50% of all UC claimants. The largest unrepresented group are non-householders and those exempt from CT liability. (p11)
Report: Hidden holes: Deductions and sanctions for people on Universal Credit
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: TA cost information patchy
Topics: Misc
Headline: Existing information on TA costs is “patchy”.
Questions: How does patchiness distort policy choices?
Impact: Weak evidence base undermines cost-effectiveness assessments.
Evidence: “Despite this, the information that exists on the costs of TA is patchy. This lack of reliable information on the cost of TA makes it challenging to develop homelessness policy that is truly based on evidence.” – p. 10
Report: Spending on Temporary Accommodation…
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact

Name: HBAI misstates poverty under heterogeneous inflation
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: HBAI misstates poverty rates when inflation is felt very differently across the income distribution
Questions: How would applying household-specific inflation rates change estimates of income change, poverty and deprivation?
Impact: Requires a relatively complicated construction of an alternative cost index to account for the heterogeneous experiences of inflation.
Evidence: “In this section, we consider two measurement issues in the HBAI data that limit our ability to analyse changes in poverty and deprivation and what drives them. The first is the use of aggregate, rather than household-specific, inflation measures when calculating real household incomes…” (p 27) The authors then go on to use data from the Living Costs and Food Survey to produce adjusted poverty estimates after adjusting for household-specific costs.  But they then add: “Given the data demands and conceptual challenges of conducting this exercise … it is not clear that this approach should be routinely adopted in income statistics.” (p 32)
Report: Living standards, poverty and inequality in the UK: 2024
Author: IFS
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: FRS underestimates benefits receipt
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: There is under-reporting of benefits in FRS data
Questions: To what extent are low-income households actually receiving benefits, and how has this changed over time?
Impact: Precise level of benefits receipt is hard to measure with FRS, even though we can assess changes over time
Update: DWP have been working on using benefits administrative data to replace survey questions about benefits.  Updated datasets since 2018/19 were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence: We know that there is under-reporting of benefits within the Family Resources Survey, therefore the following values likely overstate the proportion of households not in receipt of benefits. However, comparing this value in 1994–97 to the value in 2020–23 is likely to show an accurate picture of change over time, especially given the scale of the changes. The proportion of households receiving no income from social security rose from 16% of households in decile 1 in 1994–97 to 41% of households in 2020–23. In decile 2 we see that it rose from 6% of households in 1994–97 to 33% in 2020-23. (p45)
Report: Poverty in Scotland 2024
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: EU-SILC child deprivation relies on adult proxies
Topics: Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Adult proxy responses to EU-SILC material deprivation questions mask differences between individual children and between children and parents.
Questions: Measuring individual and child-level material deprivation
Impact: Risk of mis-measuring child deprivation levels and overlooking within-household variation
Evidence: “Secondly, the EU-SILC questions are addressed to a respondent who answers on behalf of children in the household as a group, obscuring any differences there may be between different children within the household… The EU-SILC child deprivation data reflects the perspective of adult respondents.” (p14)
Report: Living arrangements, intra-household inequality and children’s deprivation: Evidence from EU-SILC
Author: LSE CASE - (Burchardt & Karagiannaki)
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intra-Household Inequality); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Housing Executive lacks repeat TA user needs data
Topics: Misc
Headline: Lack of systematic collection of needs-related data limit institutional understanding of why people repeatedly move in and out of temporary accommodation
Questions: Why do some people re-enter TA multiple times, and what interventions would break the cycle?
Impact: Hinders design of targeted prevention & support programmes for high-repeat users.
Evidence:
“Front-line staff acknowledged that there were gaps in their understanding of why people approach them for assistance … A particular area … is around the reasons why some single people, especially males, repeatedly move in and out of temporary accommodation.” (p 34)

“There are also potential gaps in the Housing Executive’s understanding of the reasons why people require temporary accommodation on a given day, and what their accommodation circumstances were the previous night.” (p 35)
Report: Value for Money in Homelessness: A spotlight on temporary accommodation in Northern Ireland
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact

Name: Official child poverty statistics at the local level are before-housing-costs only
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Official child-poverty statistics at the local level are produced before housing costs only, forcing researchers to construct their own AHC series
Questions: What is child poverty after housing costs at local-authority level?
Impact: Sufficiently granular data on poverty after accounting for housing costs that are desired by researchers have to be constructed by academics outside of the government departments.  This could result in sustainability concerns and accessibility limitations compared to the statistics being produced by departments themselves.
Evidence: “Child poverty is defined as the share of children under 16 in low-income families… These statistics from DWP are reported on a before-housing-cost basis. Here we use the after-housing-cost measure developed by researchers at Loughborough University, using administrative data on rents for local authorities combined with household-level data from the Understanding Society survey to adjust the official DWP before-housing-cost measure of poverty.” (p 13)
Report: Uneven ground: Assessing the state of UK geographic economic inequality facing the new Government
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Flawed pension valuation distorts household wealth estimates
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Flawed methodology for valuing pensions creates faulty estimates of household wealth.
Questions: How much wealth do UK households have?
Impact: The only official source of information regarding the distribution of household wealth will be less useful for calculating household wealth totals and distribution because pensions are not being valued in a way that is consistent with other assets.
Evidence:
"The revision results from three changes in methodology made by the ONS to its Wealth andAssets Survey (WAS), which aims to record the level and distribution of wealth of Britishhouseholds every two years....But the third change – by far the biggest, by itself subtracting a staggering £2.3 trillion fromrecorded wealth – is misguided, resulting from jumbled economic reasoning. The change matters not only because of its colossal magnitude but because, while data on income are comparativelyplentiful (not least from tax records), WAS represents practically the only official source ofinformation regarding the distribution of household wealth in Great Britain. It is quite possiblethat the ONS’s revised estimates of household wealth are actually further from the truth than theestimates it started with (themselves undoubtedly flawed)." (p5)

"Implicitly, in other words, market interest rates already underpin the valuation of all otherhousehold assets recorded in WAS. Using a constant SCAPE rate instead – severing the link tomarket rates – thus means that WAS values DB and DC pensions in payment, as well as futureDB pension rights, in a way that is inconsistent with all other assets. As the GovernmentActuary’s Department (GAD) makes clear in its advice to the ONS (Morley and Madden, 2024),the use of the GDP-based SCAPE rate results in a valuation that bears ‘no relation’ to that ofother assets in WAS. That throws serious doubt on the comparability of the DB pension valuescalculated to the measured values of other assets and limits the usefulness of the dataset forcalculating household wealth totals." (p12)
Report: £2 trillion poorer than previously thought? Assessing changes to household wealth statistics
Author: IFS
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Population Groups (Other); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Wealth & Assets)

Name: Car insurance claims data missing
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Data on car insurance claims is not available, so regulators and researchers cannot establish whether higher premiums for low-income or ethnically diverse areas are justified, risking continuation of the poverty premium.
Questions: Are individuals from more deprived areas suffering a "car insurance price premium"?
Impact: Regulators and researchers can’t establish whether higher premiums for low-income or ethnically diverse areas are justified, risking continuation of the poverty premium
Evidence:
"The results of this analysis suggest a need for Government to ‘get under the
bonnet’ of how car insurers are currently operating their pricing structures. A useful first step therefore would be for the Financial Conduct Authority to use its powers to request data from insurers, which would allow it to assess the extent to which current pricing structures represent fair value for consumers, including those on low incomes, as well as how the market is driving up premiums for people on low incomes and/or with protected characteristics, such as race.This data should include not just information on quotes/prices paid by different types of consumers in different areas (a larger-scale version of the analysis undertaken here) but should also include data on insurance claims made by consumers. The latter is the missing piece of the puzzle in the analysis undertaken in this report, as it would better allow for an assessment of whether insurance pricing reflects the actual ‘risk’ of insuring consumers in different areas. It should be noted though that even if it is established that pricing does reflect risk, there is a secondary question of the extent to which this practice is ethical and whether it violates the fundamental purpose of insurance to share the burden of risk more evenly across the population." (p6)
Report: Driving up costs
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Long-term data pooling needed for subgroup transitions
Topics: Poverty Characteristics; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Small subgroup analysis of transitions in and out of very deep poverty using Understanding Society requires long-term data pooling.
Questions: How can we robustly understand the experiences of smaller population groups in very deep poverty?
Impact: Subgroup analysis requires pooling of many waves of survey data, which limits the timeliness / relevance / precision of the analysis
Evidence: So far, the large sample size available for analysis in each wave of Understanding Society has allowed us to estimate the overall proportion, and number, of people who moved into and out of very deep poverty and who lived in persistent very deep poverty in the latest four waves of data only. However, to gain more robust insights into the experiences of smaller groups of people across the population, data from the past 10 waves of Understanding Society has been pooled for the following analyses. (p8)
Report: The dynamics of very deep poverty in the UK
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Transitions & Drivers); Population Groups (Other); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty)

Name: DWP Income Dynamics omits very deep poverty and its drivers
Topics: Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Headline: DWP Income Dynamics Publication tracks general poverty transitions but not very deep poverty or its drivers
Questions: Various relating to transitions into and out of poverty
Impact: We may miss important aspects of deep poverty from a longitudinal perspective.
Evidence: While the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) has estimated the number of people who move in and out of poverty each year, their work has focused on the wider group of people in overall poverty (with incomes less than 60% of the UK median, after housing costs). They estimate that 8% of people not in poverty moved into poverty between 2019–20 and 2020–21 (this is equivalent to around 4.2 million people) while 39% of people in poverty moved out of poverty (around 5.2 million people). They also found that 12% of the UK population (around 7.9 million people) had lived in poverty for at least three out of the four years up to 2020–21 (DWP, 2023b). But their analysis does not offer any insights into very deep poverty or the events that lead to it. (p4)
Report: The dynamics of very deep poverty in the UK
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Transitions & Drivers); Poverty Characteristics (Deep Poverty)

Name: No shared LWA evaluation approach across councils
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: No shared evaluation approach for Local Welfare Assistance (LWA) across councils; each council takes its own approach and has its own goals, which limits evaluation and comparison across LAs.
Questions: How can councils consistently evaluate LWA effectiveness and compare schemes?
Impact: Inconsistent methods and limited datasets prevent robust cross?council learning or benchmarking.
Evidence:
“There is currently no agreed mechanism for evaluation of local welfare provision
across councils. There are several challenges to a cross-council approach to
evaluation. Firstly, desired outcomes are set locally. Secondly, the LWA
administrative data held by a council is designed to meet individual local
requirements, and these are primarily budgetary. This evaluation framework seeks to provide common evaluation questions relevant to all LWA schemes, and to suggest a framework for data capture requirements.” (p19)
Report: Evaluation of Local Welfare Assistance: Final framework and research findings
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Lack of data sharing prevents Council Tax Relief auto-enrolment for fluctuating earners
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Current DWP and LA data-sharing arrangements don't let councils identify households with fluctuating earnings, which prevents them from establishing auto enrolment and re-enrolment procedures for CTR
Questions: How can CTR schemes be better designed to support low-income households?
Impact: Missed entitlements; higher arrears; barriers to returning to work. (Policy in Practice)
Evidence:
"Better data sharing between DWP and local authorities: Under the current data
sharing arrangements local authorities lose visibility over households with
fluctuating earnings. If the household loses employment, the CTR claim doesn't
automatically resume and households often don’t know they need to reapply for
CTR. This manual process prevents households from accessing support and may
create barriers for people to return to the workforce." (p5)
Report: A path to better Council Tax Support schemes
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Poverty Characteristics (Fluctuating Earnings); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Household-level data obscures individual economic vulnerability
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Economic data is generally collected at household level, obscuring individual-level circumstances and vulnerabilities
Questions: Questions around individuals' cirumstances
Impact: Women's poverty and financial dependence is invisible in national statistics
Evidence: "Use of household-level data – income is not shared equally within households: household-based economic measurements obscure women’s individual financial situations and economic vulnerability, making women’s poverty and financial dependence invisible in national statistics." (p3)
Report: Gender, data and the labour market:Why data collection is not gender neutral
Author: Women's Budget Group
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intra-Household Inequality); Geographic Scope (UK); Population Groups (Women & Gender); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Scottish actual rent data poorly measured
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Geographic Scope
Headline: Actual rents paid by tenants in Scotland are not measured well, forcing researchers to focus only on rents for new tenancies.
Questions: Are LHA rates adequate for meeting the costs of housing?
Impact: Analysis of LHA adequacy must be limited to new tenancies, potentially missing a large segment of the population
Evidence:
"Data on the actual rents that tenants are paying across the private rental sector in Scotland are not measured well, meaning analysis of rents in the private rental sector is difficult and needs to be treated with some caution. However, there is data available on new rents in Scotland, so in that context we can explore the adequacy of LHA for a household seeking a new tenancy.

Newly advertised rents are what private landlords are asking for, per month, in this case on the property search engine Rightmove. These rents tend to be higher and more volatile than rents across the whole private sector as they only measure the rent on new tenancies. Landlords tend to use changing tenants as an opportunity to raise rents. This is even more so in recent years when they are reacting to inflation, increased interest rates and the relaxation of rent controls, introduced during the pandemic and unwound by the end of March 2024." (p64)
Report: Poverty in Scotland 2024
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Disability cost estimates uncertain
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups
Headline: Uncertain estimates of the additional costs of disability hinder accurate comparison of living standards
Questions: How can we adjust income measures to account for the varied additional costs faced by disabled people?
Impact: The researchers chose to exclude income from disabled benefits, but both approaches are less accurate than having accurate cost estimates
Evidence: “A key analytical question is how to compare the living standards of disabled and non-disabled people given these additional costs. One approach might be to subtract these additional costs from a measure of disposable income, in the same way that the after-housing-costs measure of income subtracts spending on housing costs. Indeed, some research has attempted to estimate these additional costs, but the estimates are uncertain, and do not reflect the wide variation in costs within the population.” (p5)
Report: Costly differences: Living standards for working-age people with disabilities
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Population Groups (Disability); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: Longitudinal food bank impact data missing
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Lack of longitudinal data limits ablity to assess long-term impact of food bank advice services.
Questions: What are the long-term impacts of food bank advice services?
Impact: Can't assess long-term impact of intervention
Evidence:
"Longitudinal research. To gain a much greater understanding of the
outcomes from food bank advice services over the medium to long term (e.g.
is there a fall in levels of destitution over time); what works in terms of achieving positive outcomes over time, and for who, longitudinal research
would be needed. A longitudinal approach, with the intervention group and
with a comparator group, could also help to disentangle the impacts of food
bank advice services from other factors, and to further test the validity of
existing findings. Studies of this nature require substantial time and resources." (p110-111)
Report: Evaluating money matters advice and support services provided through food banks
Author: University of Bristol Personal Finance Research Centre
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Food Security)

Name: Irregular income sources excluded from household income
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Irregular sources of income are not included in standard definitions of household income
Questions: Measuring relative incomes across the population while accounting for all sources of income
Impact: Relative poverty may not be accurately measured if certain sources of income are not included.  However, this may be mitigated by the fact that income from wealth will be disproportionately on the top end of the distribution (and therefore less impactful on the median income used to calculate relative poverty thresholds), and the authors note that in other work they have found ways to measure this income, albeit in roundabout ways
Evidence:
"There are some notable omissions from standard definitions of household income. As we have explored in previous work, all capital gains, pension lump sums, inheritances and gifts are excluded on the basis that they are ‘irregular’. These are significant choices that affect the relative incomes of different groups, trends across time and comparisons across countries. In the case of capital gains, this is true of taxable capital gains, but including the (untaxed) capital gains on main residences in estimates of income would have an even larger impact on such statistics." (p17)

NB: This sort of gap is explored further in other RF research: <https://www.resolutionfoundation.org/publications/who-gains/>
Report: The Living Standards Audit 2022
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Wealth & Assets)

Name: Unpaid care excluded from GDP
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Unpaid care and its value is not measured in GDP
Questions: What is the contribution of unpaid care to household and individual-level economic statistics?
Impact: Women's economic contributions are understated
Evidence:
"Measurement of unpaid work – Women do 50% more unpaid work than men, yet
official economic indicators often exclude this contribution4. For example, women’s
unpaid childcare alone is worth an estimated £140 billion annually, yet it remains
invisible in GDP calculations." (p3)
Report: Gender, data and the labour market:Why data collection is not gender neutral
Author: Women's Budget Group
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Women & Gender); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Savings/debt data limited and delayed
Sources: FRS / HBAI; Financial Lives Survey; Wealth and Assets Survey
Topics: Economic Resources
Headline: Analysis of savings and debt for low income families had to rely on JRF's own cost of living tracker data, since HBAI savings data are limited, and it and other datasets covering this topic are not timely.
Questions: What do debt and savings look like for the poorest households?
Impact: Third-sector data had to be used to fill the gap. This is probably less accurate than if official statistics were available
Evidence:
"This analysis mainly focuses on levels of savings and debt in households
with very low incomes rather than strictly households in poverty. It draws
on limited data from HBAI on savings levels and primarily uses our JRF
cost of living tracker. This is because we are limited in the lack of data,
particularly up-to-date data, that allows us to investigate this.
The Wealth and Assets Survey for 2020–2022 had not yet been published
at the time of writing, and the Financial Conduct Authority’s (FCA’s)
Financial Lives Survey (FLS) that we drew on in our 2024 report has not yet
been updated." (p112)
Report: UK Poverty 2025
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Economic Resources & Living Standards (Wealth & Assets)

Name: FRS undercounts UC recipients
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: FRS undercounts benefit recipients, so the number of children thought not to receive Universal Credit (UC) is likely overstated.
Questions: What are the rates of households in poverty that do not receive Universal Credit?
Impact: Suggests that poverty rates may be overstated since benefit claims aren't necessarily being captured through the survey data.
Update: DWP have been working on using benefits administrative data to replace survey questions about benefits.  Updated datasets since 2018/19 were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence: “It should be noted that the number of children not receiving UC complicates our understanding of child poverty in Scotland, as the underlying survey – the Family Resources Survey – is known to undercount the number of people receiving benefits, whether due to under-reporting among survey recipients or other methodological challenges. ...this means the estimate of the number of children in poverty who do not receive UC likely over-states the true number…” (p19)
Report: Achieving the 2030 child poverty target: The distance left to travel
Author: IPPR Scotland
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Intersectionality & Subgroups of Census data is limited at local level
Topics: Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Intersectionality & Subgroups is limited at local level by statistical disclosure control in Census 2021.
Questions: Various intersectional analyses at local levels
Impact: Small-area analysis is often not possible using publicly available Census data.
Evidence: Detailed intersectional local data is limited by Statistical Disclosure Control (SDC), designed to prevent the identification of individuals. This may hinder those searching for very detailed information within a small geographic area. Records may be swapped between small areas or not released at all. This means that researchers using open access census data may need to choose between searching a more detailed geography or using more detailed variables in order to see any results. Whilst SDC is a crucial tool for protecting individual identities, this is nonetheless a limitation for those who support people based on specific characteristics (for example, a small ethnic population of women) within their local area. (p8)
Report: The Census and Feminism: How Census 2021 Data Benefits Feminist Analysis and Activism
Author: Women’s Budget Group & Global Institute for Women’s Leadership
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Geographic Scope (Local Authority)

Name: NRPF poverty data severely limited
Topics: Population Groups
Headline: Limited data on people subject to NRPF and poverty rates by immigration status restricts understanding of how the status interacts with poverty
Questions: What proportion of households subject to NRPF are living in poverty?
Impact: Understanding of impact of NRPF on poverty is limited
Evidence: “The availability and interpretation of data play a crucial role in policymaking. But there is limited data on people subject to the NRPF condition and on poverty rates by immigration status. This hinders the development of targeted interventions and policies. Without accurate data, policymakers cannot fully comprehend the scale of the issue, leading to the exclusion of children with migrant parents facing poverty from key policy considerations.” (p22)
Report: Hidden Hardships: The immigration system and child poverty
Author: IPPR
Topics_Text: Population Groups (Migrants)

Name: Poor local homelessness data hinders intervention evaluation
Topics: Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Poor-quality and fragmented local homelessness data, plus weak linkage across government, impede evaluation of interventions.
Questions: What is the relative impact and cost-effectiveness of homelessness interventions, especially preventative ones? How can data linkage be improved?
Impact: Limits the ability to judge which interventions work and to allocate resources efficiently.
Evidence: The challenges associated with the quality and completeness of much of the dataheld by local areas on homelessness trends, and the difficulties in matching or cross-referring this with data held on the same people elsewhere in government, presentanother formidable barrier. While there is a good body of qualitative evidenceilluminating the key drivers and lived experiences of homelessness, we lack a solid base of rigorous causal evidence regarding the relative impact, and cost effectiveness,of homelessness interventions, particularly for preventative interventions whoseimpact may not, by their nature, be apparent until a period of years has elapsed. (p6-7)
Report: A smarter approach to homelessness
Author: Institute for Government & Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: Welsh education data lack direct parental income
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Parental income is not directly linked to children in education, which forces researchers to proxy low incomes to assess relationships between poverty and educational attainment.
Questions: What is the educational attainment gap between children in and out of poverty?
Impact: Low income must be proxied using FSM eligibility rather than more accurate data
Evidence:
"Education is a devolved power, which means that education systems vary
across the 4 countries in the UK. Attainment in Wales is therefore not
directly comparable to any of the other UK nations. In most cases, schools
and governments do not collect information on the incomes of parents, so
we need to use other information that is available to identify when children
might be growing up in a household in poverty or with low/lower incomes." (p70)
Report: Poverty in Wales 2025
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Transitions & Drivers); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Education); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: FRS material deprivation index is outdated [NB: see contextual update]
Topics: Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Headline: FRS material-deprivation index has not been updated since 2010/11, so doesn't capture changes in social standards (such as digital exclusion since internet access is now standard)
Questions: Has material deprivation increased or decreased over time?
Impact: New forms of hardship (ie lack of internet) go un-measured.
Update: Since publication of the cited report, DWP have introduced some changes to the material deprivation measures in FRS.  A technical report of these changes is available here: https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/households-below-average-income-for-financial-years-ending-1995-to-2024/technical-report-update-to-measures-using-material-deprivation-for-households-below-average-income-fye-2024
Evidence: In addition, it should be notedthat the list of items included in the FRS material deprivation indicator wasfixed in 2010/11 and was not revised during the 2010s. As a result, whileitems such as access to internet or digital equipment might haveincreasingly been viewed as essential or typical as the decade progressed,these items are not captured or reflected (p16)
Report: Going backwards? The slowdown, stalling and reversal of progress in reducing child poverty in Britain during the second decade of the 21st century
Author: LSE CASE – Vizard et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Poverty Characteristics (Material Deprivation)

Name: LWA datasets lack cross-council matching identifiers
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: LWA admin datasets often lack identifiers for matching to other council datasets, blocking linkage and holistic evaluation.
Questions: How can councils consistently evaluate LWA effectiveness and compare schemes?
Impact: Limits longitudinal and cross?service analysis, weakening evidence on outcomes.
Evidence:
"Most LWA administrative datasets examined for this project were not designed for
evaluation of service provision, or for providing a holistic view of resident support
across the council. In a number of councils, LWA datasets do not contain any
reference that would allow for matching with other council datasets, such as benefits, council tax, or arrears. This risks data on LWA provision being isolated from data on other council services and hinders a holistic approach to the resident and their support needs." (p19)
Report: Evaluation of Local Welfare Assistance: Final framework and research findings
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Surveys can't measure within-year income volatility
Topics: Economic Resources; Poverty Characteristics; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Surveys are limited for understanding within-year income volatility; lack of accessible admin data means this subject is very hard to study.
Questions: Various questions about income volatility
Impact: Within-year income volatility remains not fully understood
Evidence: “Few studies map the impact of income volatility on UC households, because until now it wasn’t possible. Most data sources available to researchers cannot tell us anything about within-year income volatility. Survey data is limited in how much they tell us how frequently people receive different types of income.” (p6)
Report: Cheques and imbalances: How income varies throughout the year for Universal Credit households
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Poverty Characteristics (Fluctuating Earnings); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: RO4 data suffer reliability and reporting gaps
Topics: Misc
Headline: RO4 (expenditure returns data submitted by LAs to central government) data suffer from reliability issues and reporting gaps.
Questions: How much do LAs spend on temporary accomodation, and how do they pay for this?
Impact: True scale of spending on temporary accomodation is obscured
Evidence: “RO4 data in England gives cause for concern about its reliability, due to the level of variation in reported costs by local authorities for the same types of TA… Not all local authorities in England consistently submit information on TA in either their RO4 or H-Clic returns. As a result, figures for these authorities have to be imputed, or may be missing altogether.... The costs of temporary accommodation are met from a variety of sources... The proportion of TA costs paid for from these different sources is difficult to establishin detail from published information.” – pp. 15-16
Report: Spending on Temporary Accommodation…
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact

Name: Housing Executive rent data collected manually only
Topics: Misc
Headline: Housing Executive collects data on rents it collects manually, severely limiting usability.
Questions: How much rent is collected and where are arrears concentrated?
Impact: Unable to forecast revenue, manage arrears or test rent-collection strategies.
Evidence:
"There are gaps in the information the Housing Executive holds about the rents it
is able to collect from people experiencing homelessness placed into temporary
accommodation. Temporary accommodation residents have to contribute to rent eitherfrom their own income or from Housing Benefit, or both. The Housing Executive is, inmany cases, responsible for collecting rent from people experiencing homelessness
residing in temporary accommodation. Currently, however, data on this is gathered
manually, and not automated, due to the Housing Executive’s IT systems. As a
result, the Housing Executive is not able to easily state how much income it collects
or its level of rent arrears. The Housing Executive acknowledged that very little
rent is currently collected from people placed in non-standard types of temporary
accommodation. It was also not able to tell us how much rent it is collecting overall,
or what its target levels for this are. Additionally, the Housing Executive’s systems do not allow it to easily track costs across different geographic areas. The transfer of this system to an IT system is currently being explored." (p33)
Report: Value for Money in Homelessness: A spotlight on temporary accommodation in Northern Ireland
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact

Name: Welsh policy analysis relies on two LA datasets
Topics: Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Reliance on a few LA admin datasets for benefits means certain populations are not covered by policy analysis in Wales
Questions: What is the impact of a variety of policy options on poverty in Wales?
Impact: Reliance on LA benefits admin data means certain groups are not properly covered by the policy analysis
Evidence: “This analysis uses local authority benefit administration data. This data holds household characteristics and income for low-income households in receipt of Council Tax Support or Housing Benefit from two Welsh council areas. It has coverage of low-income householders (those with a Council Tax liability) but excludes non-householders (e.g., those living in the household of a relative or friend). In addition, households that have income that is too high for either Council Tax Reduction or Housing Benefit, as well as those who have not applied for benefit support, are not represented. Some of the households not represented within the data may also be in poverty. The data, therefore, represents a subset of those in poverty, the lowest-income householders" (p14)
Report: An examination of the impact of policy interventions designed to reduce poverty in Wales
Author: Policy in Practice
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations)

Name: FRS/HBAI do not reflect real disability and childcare costs
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: FRS / HBAI don't account for real costs incurred by those with disabilities or caring duties, or the actual consumption patterns of households
Questions: Measuring household incomes
Impact: Some households' incomes will be inaccurately measured / estimated
Evidence: The standard definition of income used in this report does notadjust for the extra costs associated with disability and does not deductfrom income benefits such as Disability Living Allowance, AttendanceAllowance and Personal Independence Payments which are made inrecognition of these additional costs. Childcare costs are also not deductedfrom income. The deflators used for price adjustment measure priceinflation based on overall patterns of household consumption, and do notadjust for the specific consumption patterns of households at the bottomof the income distribution. (p34)
Report: Going backwards? The slowdown, stalling and reversal of progress in reducing child poverty in Britain during the second decade of the 21st century
Author: LSE CASE – Vizard et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Disability); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: OBR lacks policy-specific fiscal multipliers
Topics: Misc
Headline: OBR lacks capacity for policy-specific fiscal multipliers, which limits quantification of economic impacts of anti-poverty policies
Questions: What are the fiscal impacts of scrapping the two-child limit?
Impact: Anti-poverty policies may be misevaluated in economic forecasting, and therefore go overlooked as viable policy options
Evidence: “The current ability for OBR to tailor their multipliers to specific policies is limited. Pragmatically, the OBR lacks capacity to make bespoke multiplier estimates for every policy and is therefore limited to existing evidence which tends to generalise over large areas of policy.” (p 15)
Report: Capping ambitions
Author: New Economics Foundation

Name: Gender-disaggregated benefit cap data is not publicly available
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Gender-disaggregated data on who is affected by the benefit cap are not easily available
Questions: What is the gender distribution of households affected by the benefit cap?
Impact: Gendered impact of benefits cap policy cannot be assessed with precision
Evidence: The number of families hit by the [benefit] cap falls steadily as the age of the youngest child increasesand work becomes more accessible for parents, with the high cost of childcare becoming lessof a barrier. There are 57,000 families with a child under five impacted by the cap. Recentbenefit cap data by gender is not easily available, but the vast majority of those impacted aresingle mothers. (p9)
Report: Capping ambitions
Author: New Economics Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Women & Gender); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Child Benefit admin data provides ethnicity only in aggregated categories
Topics: Economic Resources; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Child Benefit admin data provide ethnicity only in highly aggregated categories.
Questions: Which specific minority ethnic groups bear the greatest policy burden?
Impact: Masks differences for subcategories
Evidence: A note on ethnicity categories: The administrative data for Child Benefit,which we use to provide our estimate of the demographic make-up of thepopulation of children, is provided using highly aggregated categories ofethnicity; we do not have access to further breakdowns within groups. TheFRS data allows greater nuance, in particular in relation to the Asiancategory, but still requires grouping some categories together where thereare likely to be substantive differences (notably Black African, BlackCaribbean and Black British are combined; and non-British white aremerged into the ‘Other’ category). (p8-9)
Report: Baking inequality in from birth:New analysis of the way the two-childlimit and the benefit cap intersect withethnicity
Author: LSE CASE - Stewart et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Intersectionality & Subgroups); Population Groups (Ethnic Minorities); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: London-wide rough-sleeping database is not connected to other data systems
Topics: Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: CHAIN rough-sleeping database is not connected to borough, health or justice systems, hampering outcome tracking
Questions: What additional insights would full integration of CHAIN with other public datasets provide on the effectiveness of rough-sleeping interventions?
Impact: City-wide impact of programmes cannot be measured; resources may be misallocated
Evidence:
"CHAIN: database commissioned by the Mayor
of London and records information about people
sleeping rough across London which allows
more detailed monitoring of patterns of rough
sleeping across London than would be possible using individual borough systems, and assists in provision of services to people who move between different parts of London.  Shortcomings: the system currently lacks
integration or full data sharing with borough systems, health systems and the criminal justicesystem, making it difficult to track the impact ofinterventions on reducing rough sleeping." (p31)
Report: Pan-Approaches to Tackling Homelessness in London and Other Megacities
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: Duty to Refer processes unstandardised across LAs
Topics: Essential Services; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Duty to Refer' process is complicated by lack of standardisation of referal process across LAs and data protection regulations.
Questions: NA
Impact: Reduces the effectiveness of homeless interventions and research
Evidence:
"Local authority autonomy in applying homelessness and rough sleeping
guidance can also complicate collaborative working across agencies.
Interviewees noted this as a particular issue within the Duty to Refer
process. For example, MHCLG developed a template referral
form for the Duty to Refer process, but some local authorities have
developed their own mechanisms for processing referrals, which other
public bodies cannot always access. In some cases, to process a referral,
local authorities request certain information, which referring bodies are not
always able to provide due to data protection. Furthermore, local authorities
are not obliged to update referring bodies on the progress of the referral." (p42)
Report: Systems-wide evaluation of homelessness and rough sleeping: preliminary findings
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact; RSM; Cordis Bright
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness)

Name: Homelessness data can't disaggregate intervention types
Topics: Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Existing data on homelessness interventions in Greater Manchester cannot disaggregate different forms of homelessness or link young care-leavers to the specific interventions they receive
Questions: Do “A Bed Every Night”, Housing First, and the Young Persons Prevention Pathfinder reduce homelessness and improve accommodation outcomes for care-leavers in Greater Manchester?
Impact: Prevents a detailed understanding of how homelessness is changing, which limits our ability to evaluate policy effectiveness
Evidence: The study uses a difference-in-differences approach to evaluate the impact of the aforementioned interventions on homelessness outcomes of care leavers, asrecorded in data from the local authority’s looked-after census records in GreaterManchester. It should be noted that this data does not allow us to distinguish between people experiencing different forms of homelessness — for example, rough sleeping, temporary accommodation, or emergency accommodation." p11)
Report: Investigating the Impacts of Housing Interventions on Homelessness among Care Leavers in Greater Manchester
Author: Centre for Homelessness Impact (CHI) & Greater Manchester Combined Authority
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Policy Design & Evaluation); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Population Groups (Other)

Name: FRS education categories changed due to an error that had been identified with previous versions of the relevant question, breaking time series
Topics: Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Changing education categories in the FRS break long-run series
Questions: How does parental education relate to poverty risk over time?
Impact: Researchers had to resort to proxying educational attainment of parents, reducing confidence / precision in analysis
Evidence: Educational classification categories in the FRS have changed several times, making a consistent long-term series difficult to construct. Figure 2 therefore shows the age at which parents completed their full-time education, which has been collected every year since 1994/95. This gives us a rough proxy for educational qualifications, showing the percentage of children’s whose (longest educated) parent left education before 18 (unlikely to have gained A levels or equivalent), and the percentage with a parent who finished education at 21 or older (likely having gained a university degree or equivalent). (p13)
Report:
A time of need: Exploring the changing
poverty risk facing larger families in
the UK
Author: LSE CASE - Stewart et al.
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Transitions & Drivers); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Education); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Scottish childhood poverty earnings penalty unanalysable
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Scottish-level analysis of childhood-poverty earnings penalty impossible because longitudinal survey (Understanding Society) sample size is too small.
Questions: What is the long-term earnings impact of childhood poverty in Scotland?
Impact: Forces reliance on UK-wide estimates
Evidence:
"Using data from the British Household Panel Survey and its successor Understanding
Society we identify individuals over the age of 30 for whom household income data
from their childhood (under the age of 16) is available. Due to the limited sample size
we analyse data gathered across the UK rather than just in Scotland." (p50)
Report: Tipping the Scales: The social and economic harm of poverty in Scotland
Author: IPPR
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Disability benefits inflate income without accounting costs
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Disability benefits are counted as income without a corresponding accounting of costs, which leads to an underestimation of people with disabilities in poverty.
Questions: How does counting disability benefits in household income distort poverty measurement for disabled households?
Impact: Underestimates poverty among disabled households.  However, since it is possible to conduct analysis that removes disability benefits, the impact is smaller than it could be.  Still, there is a concern that disability benefits may not fully cover disability costs, and therefore there could still be an understimation issue.
Evidence: As a result, how disability benefits are treated in calculating poverty rates is crucial. This is because poverty rates are calculated using a household’s total income from all sources including from work and benefits, crucially including disability benefits. As disability benefits are intended to cover the additional costs of being disabled, they do not contribute to a household’s disposable income; that is, the funds are not available to spend on day-to-day costs. This means that using the total household income including disability benefits overestimates the incomes of households in which someone receives disability benefits, masking the risk of poverty for those households. (p68-69)
Report: Poverty in Scotland 2024
Author: JRF
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (Devolved Nations); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Disability); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: LAs can't identify children missing health visits
Topics: Essential Services; Population Groups; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Almost all local authorities lack the data to identify children who miss their health visitor home visits and whether they are from disadvantaged families or have special educational needs
Questions: What are the characteristics of children who miss early years home visits?
Impact:
"Incomplete data makes it hard for
local authorities to identify children who would benefit from more
targeted support, and for national government to support councils
to improve the quality of their health visiting services." (p30)
Evidence:
"While there are data on how many children are receiving home visits,
there is little available to identify who specifically is not receiving
them.45 According to one recent study, only 13 per cent of local
authorities could identify whether a child who missed their review
at two to two and a half was also from a disadvantaged family, and
only 13 per cent could identify whether a child who missed a review
had special educational needs." (p30)
Report: Early Years Commission: A Cross-Party Manifesto
Author: Centre for Social Justice, Fabian Society
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Geographic Scope (Local Authority); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Children & Families)

Name: Official poverty metrics ignore childcare costs
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Population Groups; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Official poverty metrics overstate disposable income by ignoring childcare costs and by counting childcare reimbursements as income.
Questions: What do poverty rates look like after accounting for the costs of childcare?
Impact: Leads to a likely underestimate of poverty rates among families with children
Evidence:
“For those who do pay for formal childcare, the significant impact of
these costs on their residual household income is largely unaccounted for in
official estimates, as the relative poverty measure does not subtract childcare costs
when calculating disposable income. This means that working parents appear to
fare better in the data than they are in reality. Previous analysis commissioned by
Child Poverty Action Group has shown that poverty rates would be higher still for
some groups of working parents if childcare costs were accounted for in the official
poverty calculation (Hirsch and Valadez 2015). This problem is exacerbated by the
fact that any childcare reimbursement through the universal credit and tax credit
regimes is counted as income in the data, distorting the picture for these groups.” (p18)
Report: No longer ‘managing’: The rise of working poverty and fixing Britain’s broken social settlement
Author: IPPR
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Health & Care); Population Groups (Children & Families); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)

Name: HBAI misses £40bn benefits, skewing poverty metrics
Topics: Economic Resources; Geographic Scope; Analysis & Insights
Headline: HBAI survey misses ~£40 billion of benefit income each year, skewing poverty & inequality metrics
Questions: What is the poverty rate in the UK, and how bad is inequality?
Impact: Poverty won't be accurately measured without accurately accounting for benefits income
Update: DWP have been working on using benefits administrative data to replace survey questions about benefits.  Updated datasets since 2018/19 were released in 2026: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/family-resources-survey--2
Evidence:
"However, one known problem with UK data is that the surveys underestimate total benefit income relative to what we know is spent each year… Our previous work shows that over £40 billion a year is unaccounted for in the HBAI data, and this has an impact on statistics about income and inequality levels and how they have changed over time. Data improvements are expected at the DWP and ONS… with the potential to use administrative data to correct survey responses.” (p41)

NB: This gap is further explored in other RF outputs: <https://www.resolutionfoundation.org/publications/improving-our-understanding-of-uk-poverty-will-require-better-data/>
Report: The Living Standards Audit 2022
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Geographic Scope (UK); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: HBAI data is published multiple years after collection
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: HBAI data is not released in a timely manner.
Questions: How will new living standards data change our understanding of poverty?
Impact: Data are not as accurate or up-to-date as would be necessary for a clearer picture of poverty and living standards, especially if the goal is practical and responsive policymaking
Evidence: “The annual release of DWP’s Households Below Average Income (HBAI) figures is far less timely than other economic indicators and, as it is based on survey data, it is noisy. Yet, together with the ONS’s separate income survey data (expected later in Spring), it provides key insights into how living standards have changed for different groups. ”
Report: Five takeaways from new living standards data
Author: Resolution Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Employment, Income & Earnings)

Name: Comprehensive data on whether individuals are eligible to claim UC are not available
Topics: Economic Resources; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Current systems do not facilitate all eligible individuals receiving UC
Questions: How many eligible families are missing out on means-tested benefits such as Universal Credit?
Impact: Benefits uptake is lower than it otherwise should / could be
Evidence: The new payment could also be used to ensure fewer people miss out on other payments they are already entitled to. Underclaiming of means-tested benefits is a significant issue in the UK. In universal credit alone, nearly 400,000 families miss out on more than £7,000 on average annually. At the point of submitting the required personal information to claim the ‘cost of living allowance’, the new system could provide a quick assessment of whether a household might be entitled to further financial support, and invite them to submit further information to complete a claim for either universal credit or pension credit as appropriate (p16)
Report: Warm Homes, Cool Planet
Author: New Economics Foundation
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Targeting & Eligibility); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Benefits & Social Security)

Name: Mortgage interest rate data missing from HBAI
Topics: Economic Resources; Essential Services; Analysis & Insights
Headline: Missing mortgage-interest-rate data from HBAI means mortgagors’ incomes are mis-measured and the poverty impacts of rising rates cannot be assessed
Questions: How do varying mortgage interest rates and remortgaging affect poverty, financial hardship and deprivation?
Impact: Evaluating dynamics of poverty in the face of mortage-related factors is difficult or impossible
Evidence: “Regrettably, we cannot test how much of this is driven by remortgaging households because the HBAI data lack information on households’ mortgage interest rates.” (p 40) “Moreover, we demonstrate that there is large mismeasurement of mortgagors’ income which limits our ability to understand changes in income and deprivation over recent years.” (p 41) "We findsome evidence of households that recently remortgaged being more likely to be behind on bills,but we lack the data to assess this hypothesis more precisely." (p41)
Report: Living standards, poverty and inequality in the UK: 2024
Author: IFS
Topics_Text: Analysis & Insights (Poverty Measurement); Essential Services & Living Conditions (Housing & Homelessness); Economic Resources & Living Standards (Costs & Expenditure)
